Delete in reverse order — Pod first, then PVC, then StorageClass, then Secret:

```bash
kubectl delete pod pod-sample
kubectl delete pvc pvc-sample
kubectl delete sc sc-sample
kubectl delete secret secret-sample
```

:::note
On VSP One B20 series storage systems, if the PVC is cloned, you must first delete the cloned PVC before deleting the parent PVC.
:::

## PVC Error Handling

- **Creation failure:** A PVC object may be created without a corresponding PV. Delete the PVC:
  ```bash
  kubectl delete pvc <PVC-name>
  ```
- **Deletion failure:** A PVC may be deleted but the PV and storage asset (LDEV) remain. View the volume properties:
  ```bash
  kubectl describe pv <PV-name>
  ```
  Then delete the PV and the LDEV via the storage system's management interface.

## Raw Block Volume

ReadWriteMany access requires raw block volume mode. This configuration is intended specifically for raw block mode RWX to support KubeVirt deployments, such as OpenShift virtualization. It is not designed for general-purpose RWX usage. Use VSP One File for regular RWX PVCs.

```yaml title="pvc-sample-rwx-raw.yaml"
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: pvc-sample-rwx
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteMany
  volumeMode: Block
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
  storageClassName: sc-sample
```

```yaml title="pod-sample-rwx.yaml"
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: pod-sample-rwx
spec:
  containers:
    - name: my-container
      image: ubuntu
      volumeDevices:
        - name: raw-volume
          devicePath: /dev/sdx
  volumes:
    - name: raw-volume
      persistentVolumeClaim:
        claimName: pvc-sample-rwx
```

## ReadOnlyMany

To create a PersistentVolumeClaim with ReadOnlyMany, you must create the PVC from an existing PVC. Use the clone manifest from [Cloning a PVC](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/storage-classes.md#cloning-a-pvc) and specify `ReadOnlyMany` as the access mode:

```yaml title="pvc-rox-sample.yaml"
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: pvc-rox-sample
spec:
  dataSource:
    name: pvc-sample
    kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
    apiGroup: ""
  accessModes:
    - ReadOnlyMany
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
  storageClassName: sc-sample
```

:::note
This feature is not supported on VSP One SDS Block.
:::



---
url: /container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/snapshots.md
---

# Volume Snapshots

HSPC supports Kubernetes-native volume snapshots using the `VolumeSnapshot` API.

For the full snapshot configuration and commands, see [Snapshots in the Storage Classes guide](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/storage-classes.md#snapshots).

:::tip
On **OpenShift**, you can start a **VolumeSnapshotClass** workflow from the HitachiCSI tab on a StorageClass. See [OpenShift CSI Console Plugin](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/csi-console-plugin.md#create-volumesnapshotclass).
:::

## Listing Snapshots

```bash
kubectl get volumesnapshot
```

```
NAME              READYTOUSE   SOURCEPVC    SOURCESNAPSHOTCONTENT   RESTORESIZE   SNAPSHOTCLASS   SNAPSHOTCONTENT
snapshot-sample   true         pvc-sample                           1Gi           snapshotclass-sample      snapcontent-xxxxxxxx
```

## Verifying Snapshot Content

```bash
kubectl describe volumesnapshot snapshot-sample
```

## Restoring from a Snapshot

Create a new PVC with `dataSource` referencing the snapshot. See [Snapshots](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/storage-classes.md#snapshots).

## Deleting a Snapshot

```bash
kubectl delete volumesnapshot snapshot-sample
```

## Snapshots for a Stretched PVC

You can create and manage snapshots of Global Active Device (GAD) volumes used by a Stretched PVC.

### Create the VolumeSnapshotClass
```yaml title="volumesnapshotclass-stretched-sample.yaml"
apiVersion: snapshot.storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: VolumeSnapshotClass
metadata:
  name: snapshotclass-sample-stretched
driver: hspc.csi.hitachi.com
deletionPolicy: Delete
parameters:
  poolID: "1"
  serialNumber: "715035"                                                         # Required for Stretched PVC snapshots
  csi.storage.k8s.io/snapshotter-secret-name: "secret-sample-stretched"
  csi.storage.k8s.io/snapshotter-secret-namespace: "default"
```
:::note
The `serialNumber` parameter is required for Stretched PVC snapshots. Use the Secret created for the Stretched PVC (which contains both primary and secondary credentials).
:::
### Create the VolumeSnapshot
```yaml title="volumesnapshot-stretched-sample.yaml"
apiVersion: snapshot.storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: VolumeSnapshot
metadata:
  name: VolumeSnapshot-stretched
spec:
  volumeSnapshotClassName: snapshotclass-sample-stretched
  source:
    persistentVolumeClaimName: <stretched-pvc-name>
```
```bash
kubectl apply -f volumesnapshotclass-stretched-sample.yaml
kubectl apply -f volumesnapshot-stretched-sample.yaml
```



---
url: /container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/expanding-volumes.md
---

# Expanding Volumes

HSPC supports online volume expansion. The StorageClass must have `allowVolumeExpansion: true` (which is set in the sample StorageClass by default).

:::warning
Confirm completion of volume expansion with `kubectl get pvc`, which displays the updated CAPACITY. Do not shut down the OS or drain the node before volume expansion completes.
:::

:::note
Volume expansion has the following restrictions: On VSP One B20 series storage systems, you cannot expand a cloned PVC to a capacity greater than its parent volume. The minimum additional size for volume expansion is 1 GiB. The maximum additional size is 7 TiB, or a value that does not exceed the warning threshold of pool capacity — if you add more than 7 TiB, run the command again. Volume capacity cannot be reduced. If `allowVolumeExpansion` of the StorageClass is set to `false`, a PersistentVolume created with this setting cannot be expanded. The size obtained by `kubectl get pv <PV-name> -o yaml` is not updated after expansion; use `kubectl get pv <PV-name>` to see the updated CAPACITY.
:::

:::caution
Use the `kubectl` command to change the size of an LDEV created with Storage Plug-in for Containers. After you apply the patch for volume expansion, if the PVC is not attached to a Pod, you must expand the filesystem from within the node.
:::

## Procedure

### Edit the PVC to request more storage
You can expand using either `kubectl edit` or `kubectl patch`:
```bash
kubectl edit pvc pvc-sample
```
Or use `kubectl patch`:
```bash
kubectl patch pvc pvc-sample --patch \
  '{"spec":{"resources":{"requests":{"storage": "10Gi"}}}}'
```
Update the storage request to the new desired size:
```yaml
spec:
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi
```
### Verify the PVC capacity has been updated
Confirm completion by checking `CAPACITY` in the output:
```bash
kubectl get pvc pvc-sample
```
```
NAME         STATUS   VOLUME                                     CAPACITY   ACCESS MODES   STORAGECLASS   AGE
pvc-sample   Bound    pvc-cf8c6089-0386-4c39-8037-e1520a986a7d  10Gi       RWO            sc-sample      2d
```
### Verify the filesystem was expanded inside the Pod
```bash
kubectl exec -it pod-sample -- df -h /data
```

:::note
For HRPC replication volumes, expand the **secondary site first**, then the primary. See [Expanding Replication Volumes](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/expanding-volumes.md).
:::

## Expanding a Stretched PVC

You can expand storage volumes on both a Stretched PVC and a Stretched PVC with ADR.

:::note
Expansion is **not allowed** if the GAD volume has an existing snapshot. Delete the snapshot before expanding.
:::

**Before you begin:**

- Verify that the `allowVolumeExpansion` parameter in the StorageClass is set to `true`. If the parameter is set to `false`, you will not be able to expand the storage volume.
- Verify that the GAD pair is healthy and in **Pair** state during volume expansion.

Run the following command to expand an existing Stretched PVC:

```bash
kubectl patch pvc <pvc-name> --patch \
  '{"spec":{"resources":{"requests":{"storage": "16Gi"}}}}' \
  -n <namespace>
```

:::note
The storage size must be greater than the current PVC size for the expansion to succeed.
:::



---
url: /container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/uninstalling.md
---

# Upgrading and Uninstalling HSPC

## Upgrading \{#upgrading}

:::note
Before upgrading, remove any alpha/beta VolumeSnapshot CRDs if present, and upgrade the container orchestrator (OpenShift/Kubernetes) first if required. Back up the HSPC CR: `kubectl get hspc -A -o yaml > hspc-backup.yaml`.
:::


**OpenShift**

### Upgrade on OpenShift
**Via the HitachiCSI console tab (HSPC 3.18+)**\
When the optional [OpenShift CSI Console Plugin](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/csi-console-plugin.md#upgrade-hspc-from-the-console) (Technology Preview) is installed, navigate to **Storage → StorageClasses**, select an HSPC-backed StorageClass, and open the **HitachiCSI** tab. When a newer version is available, the tab shows **Click to upgrade from \<current version> to \<target version>**. Clicking it uninstalls the existing Operator and reinstalls the target version from OperatorHub automatically.
**Via the OpenShift web console**
### Delete the HSPC instance
On the **Operator Details** page for **Hitachi Storage Plug-in for Containers**, delete the HSPC CustomResource from the **Storage Plug-in for Containers** tab.
### Uninstall the Operator
Navigate to **Installed Operators** and uninstall **Hitachi Storage Plug-in for Containers**.
### Install the new version
Follow the [installation procedure](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/installation.md#installation-on-openshift) to install the new version from OperatorHub.


**Kubernetes**

### Upgrade on Kubernetes
### Delete the HSPC instance
```bash
kubectl delete -f hspc_v1_hspc.yaml
```
### Delete the Operator
```bash
kubectl delete -f hspc-operator.yaml
```
### Clone the repository and navigate to the new version
```bash
git clone https://github.com/hitachi-vantara/csi-operator-hitachi
cd csi-operator-hitachi/hspc/<new-version>/operator
```
### Deploy the new Operator
```bash
kubectl create -f hspc-operator.yaml
```
### Create the HSPC instance
```bash
kubectl create -f hspc_v1_hspc.yaml
```
### Verify HSPC is ready
```bash
kubectl get hspc -n <namespace>
```
```
NAME   READY   AGE
hspc   true    30s
```


## Re-creating HSPC

Re-creation is required in some scenarios (e.g., recovering from a misconfiguration).

### Delete all Pods using HSPC volumes
### Delete all PVCs
### Delete Storage Plug-in for Containers
See [Uninstalling HSPC](#uninstalling-hspc) below.
### Re-create HSPC
See [Installing HSPC](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/installation.md).

## Uninstalling HSPC \{#uninstalling-hspc}


**OpenShift**

### Uninstall on OpenShift
1. Delete all Pods that use volumes created by Storage Plug-in for Containers.
2. Delete the VolumeSnapshotClass, VolumeSnapshot, PersistentVolumeClaim, StorageClass, and Secret that were created for Storage Plug-in for Containers.
3. Delete the HSPC instance (CustomResource) from the Operator Details page.
4. Uninstall the Operator from **Installed Operators** in the OpenShift web console.


**Kubernetes**

### Uninstall on Kubernetes
### Delete all Pods using HSPC volumes
```bash
kubectl delete pod <pod-name>
```
### Delete HSPC-created resources
Delete the VolumeSnapshotClass, VolumeSnapshot, PersistentVolumeClaim, StorageClass, and Secret that were created for Storage Plug-in for Containers:
```bash
kubectl delete pvc <pvc-name>
kubectl delete volumesnapshot <snapshot-name>
kubectl delete volumesnapshotclass <snapshotclass-name>
kubectl delete storageclass <sc-name>
kubectl delete secret <secret-name>
```
### Delete the HSPC instance
```bash
kubectl delete -f hspc_v1_hspc.yaml
```
### Delete the Operator
```bash
kubectl delete -f hspc-operator.yaml
```
### Delete the namespace
```bash
kubectl delete -f hspc-operator-namespace.yaml
```
### Delete the image pull Secret (if applicable)
If you created a Secret to pull an image, delete that Secret as well.




---
url: /container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/telemetry.md
---

# Telemetry

HSPC includes an optional telemetry service that periodically sends usage data to Hitachi Vantara. Telemetry enables Hitachi to analyze your storage usage patterns to support business insights and analytics.

## Default Behavior

Telemetry is **enabled by default**. The collected data is visible only to the vendor (Hitachi) and is not shared with any third parties.

| Sync Interval    | Scope              |
| ---------------- | ------------------ |
| Every 10 minutes | Cluster usage data |
| Every 24 hours   | AWS dashboard data |

## Data Collected

The telemetry service collects the following information:

| Field                          | Description                                  |
| ------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------- |
| Cluster UID                    | Unique identifier for the Kubernetes cluster |
| Driver version                 | HSPC version installed                       |
| Node count                     | Number of worker nodes                       |
| Kubernetes / OpenShift version | Container orchestrator version               |
| Operating system               | OS type and version on nodes                 |
| Storage model                  | Hitachi storage system model                 |
| Storage serial number          | Storage system serial number                 |
| PV count                       | Number of PersistentVolumes managed by HSPC  |
| PV capacity                    | Total capacity of managed PersistentVolumes  |

:::note
No application data, workload content, user credentials, or personally identifiable information is collected.
:::

## Network Requirements

The telemetry service requires outbound connectivity from the cluster:

| Requirement | Detail                          |
| ----------- | ------------------------------- |
| Port        | 443 (HTTPS outbound)            |
| Protocol    | TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3              |
| Proxy       | CONNECT proxy supported         |
| DNS         | Standard cluster DNS resolution |

## Disabling Telemetry

To disable telemetry:

1. Scale down the `hspc-operator-controller-manager`:

   ```bash
   kubectl scale deployment hspc-operator-controller-manager --replicas=0 -n <namespace>
   ```

2. Edit the Telemetry ConfigMap:

   ```bash
   kubectl edit configmap hspc-csi-telemetry-config -n <namespace>
   ```

3. Set `awsEnabled` to `"false"`:

   ```yaml
   data:
     awsEnabled: "false"
   ```

4. Save the configuration changes.



---
url: /container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/csi-console-plugin.md
---

# OpenShift CSI Console Plugin (Tech Preview)

HSPC includes an optional **dynamic console plugin** for **Red Hat OpenShift** only. After installation, the OpenShift web console can show Hitachi-specific views (for example, a **HitachiCSI** tab on StorageClass resources) and workflows such as clone strategy, `VolumeSnapshotClass`, static PV helpers, support bundle download, and—when a newer Operator version is available—a **Click to upgrade** action from that tab that reinstalls the target version from OperatorHub.

Console actions mirror YAML and CLI workflows in this Quick Reference. Use **[Storage Classes](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/storage-classes.md)** for StorageClass and snapshot class manifests, **[Volume Snapshots](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/snapshots.md)** for listing and restore flow, **[Configuring HSPC](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/configuration.md)** for secrets referenced by the console, and **[Provisioning Volumes](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/provisioning.md)** for PVC and Pod examples.

## Requirements

- OpenShift Container Platform (supported HSPC release; see [Release Notes](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/release-notes.md))
- HSPC Operator installed and an HSPC instance running
- Permissions to create namespaces, Deployments, `ConsolePlugin`, and to patch `consoles.operator.openshift.io cluster` (cluster admin or equivalent)
- Access to pull the console plugin container image from the Hitachi registry (image pull Secret on the workload namespace if your cluster requires it)

## Install the console plugin

The manifest is published in the HSPC GitHub repository for this product version.

**Manifest (raw):**\
[consoleplugin-ocp-ui.yaml](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/hitachi-vantara/csi-operator-hitachi/main/hspc/v3.18.0/sample/consoleplugin-ocp-ui.yaml)

**Repository path:** `csi-operator-hitachi/hspc/<version>/sample/consoleplugin-ocp-ui.yaml`

### Obtain the manifest
First, connect to the OpenShift cluster master node and log in:
```bash
ssh <username>@<node-ip-address>
oc login <cluster-api-endpoint> --username=<username> --password=<password>
```
Then clone the repository:
```bash
git clone https://github.com/hitachi-vantara/csi-operator-hitachi
cd csi-operator-hitachi/hspc/v3.18.0/sample
```
(Use the `v<x.y.z>` directory that matches your installed HSPC version.)
### Apply the manifest
```bash
oc create -f consoleplugin-ocp-ui.yaml
```
The manifest creates a dedicated namespace, Deploys the plugin frontend, registers a `ConsolePlugin` resource, and runs a short Job that merges the plugin into the cluster console configuration.
### Verify registration
```bash
oc get consoleplugin
```
You should see a `ConsolePlugin` named `console-plugin-vsp360-dcm` (resource name from the sample manifest).
### Confirm in the web console
After the console operator reconciles, open **Storage → StorageClasses**, select an HSPC-related StorageClass, and look for the Hitachi CSI tab or panel (wording may match **HitachiCSI** or the plugin display name in the `ConsolePlugin` spec). Screenshots from the installation guide are in [Console UI](#console-ui) below.

:::note
The sample manifest references a specific plugin image tag (for example `hv-ocp-ui:v3.18.0`). Align the manifest version with your HSPC Operator version.
:::

## Upgrade HSPC from the Hitachi CSI Console \{#upgrade-hspc-from-the-console}

Starting with HSPC 3.18.0, the **guided Operator upgrade** is started from the **HitachiCSI** tab. Open **Storage → StorageClasses**, select an HSPC-backed StorageClass, and open the **HitachiCSI** tab. When a newer version of HSPC is available, the tab shows **Click to upgrade from \<current version> to \<target version>**. Clicking that button uninstalls the existing Operator and reinstalls the target version from OperatorHub automatically.

If the console plugin is **not** installed, upgrade by deleting Operator then reinstalling, as described under [Upgrading and Uninstalling HSPC](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/uninstalling.md#upgrading).

## Console UI \{#console-ui}

### HitachiCSI tab on StorageClass details \{#hitachicsi-tab-on-storageclass-details}

Open **Storage → StorageClasses**, select an HSPC-backed StorageClass, and open the **HitachiCSI** tab to view provisioner settings, pool and port IDs, secret references, the **current HSPC version**, and actions such as **Clone StorageClass**, **Create VolumeSnapshotClass**, **Create Static PV**, and **Generate Support Bundle**.

Fields such as pool ID, connection type, ports, and secret map to [StorageClass parameters](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/storage-classes.md#storageclass-settings) and [Secret fields](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/configuration.md#secret-settings) in the reference YAML.

![OpenShift StorageClass details with HitachiCSI tab selected: shows StorageClass parameters, Hitachi CSI configuration, installed version, and action buttons for clone, snapshot class, static PV, and support bundle.](/images/hspc/csi-console/hitachicsi-storageclass-tab.jpeg)
### Clone StorageClass \{#clone-storageclass}

Use the console workflow to clone an existing StorageClass and adjust name, pool, ports, secret, efficiency, filesystem, and reclaim policy before creating the new class. The result is equivalent to defining another StorageClass with the same `provisioner` and `parameters` shape as in [StorageClass Settings](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/storage-classes.md#storageclass-settings) (see the VSP / VSP One Block or VSP One SDS Block tab for your array type).

To clone a **PVC** from an existing volume (different operation), see [Cloning a PVC](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/storage-classes.md#cloning-a-pvc).

![Clone StorageClass wizard in OpenShift: form fields for new StorageClass name, pool ID, ports, secret, storage efficiency, filesystem type, and reclaim policy.](/images/hspc/csi-console/clone-storageclass-wizard.jpeg)
### Create VolumeSnapshotClass \{#create-volumesnapshotclass}

Create a `VolumeSnapshotClass` from the StorageClass context; pool ID and secret fields can be prefilled from the selected StorageClass.

For the full manifest, secret parameters, and follow-on steps (snapshot object, restore PVC), see [Snapshots and VolumeSnapshotClass](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/storage-classes.md#snapshots) and the focused [Volume Snapshots](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/snapshots.md) page.

![Create VolumeSnapshotClass dialog: name, deletion policy, pool ID, secret name and namespace, with Create and Cancel actions.](/images/hspc/csi-console/create-volumesnapshotclass.jpeg)
### Create Static PV \{#create-static-pv}

Guided form for static provisioning: serial number, StorageClass, volume nickname (`spc-` prefix), LDEV ID, capacity, PV name, reclaim policy, access mode, optional PVC creation in a namespace.

This matches [Static provisioning](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/storage-classes.md#static-provisioning): prepare the volume on the array first, then see [Creating a PV](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/storage-classes.md#creating-a-static-pv) for `volumeHandle` and `volumeAttributes`, and [Creating a PVC for static provisioning](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/storage-classes.md#creating-a-static-pvc) when binding with `volumeName`. Naming rules for host groups and volume nicknames are in [Overview](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/overview.md#host-group-and-iscsi-target-naming-rules).

![Create Static PV dialog: storage system serial, StorageClass, volume nickname with Generate button, LDEV ID, capacity, PV name, reclaim policy, access mode, volume mode, filesystem, and optional PVC creation.](/images/hspc/csi-console/create-static-pv.jpeg)
### Generate Support Bundle \{#generate-support-bundle}

The console can collect logs and configuration for troubleshooting. For manual log collection, CSI log bundle tooling, and what to send to support, see [Troubleshooting](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/troubleshooting.md), [Appendix — CSI log collection](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/appendix.md#csi-log-collection), and optionally the community [CSI Log Bundle](/container-plugins/csi-log-bundle.md) page.

## Uninstall

Remove the resources created from the same manifest file:

```bash
oc delete -f consoleplugin-ocp-ui.yaml
```

If the console still lists the plugin, remove it from the cluster console operator’s plugin list per your OpenShift version documentation, or re-apply a patched `Console` resource that omits this plugin.

# Troubleshooting

## Collecting Information for Support

### Required Information

| Information                         | How to Collect                                                         |
| ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Kubernetes/OpenShift cluster info   | `kubectl cluster-info dump -A > dump.txt`                              |
| HSPC logs — csi-controller          | See [Collecting HSPC Logs](#collecting-logs)                           |
| HSPC logs — csi-node                | See [Collecting HSPC Logs](#collecting-logs)                           |
| HSPC manifests                      | YAML files used to install HSPC                                        |
| Secret, StorageClass, PVC manifests | YAML files for storage resources                                       |
| Application manifests               | YAML files for applications using HSPC volumes                         |
| Storage dump (VSP/VSP One Block)    | SVP normal dump (if using SVP), or system dump via maintenance utility |
| Storage info (VSP One SDS Block)    | System dump via maintenance utility                                    |

**Support contact:** [support.hitachivantara.com](https://support.hitachivantara.com/en_us/contact-us.html)

:::tip
For an **automated** log bundle (multiple pods, cluster context, and a browser-based viewer), see [Hitachi CSI Log Bundle](/container-plugins/csi-log-bundle.md). It is community-maintained and not a Hitachi Vantara product.

The CSI log collection tool is also available at `csi-operator-hitachi/log_collector/hspc/Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle/get_hitachicsilogs.sh` (and a PowerShell variant) in the GitHub repository.

On **OpenShift**, if the [CSI console plugin](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/csi-console-plugin.md) is installed, **Generate Support Bundle** from the HitachiCSI StorageClass view is another way to collect diagnostics (Technology Preview).
:::

## Collecting HSPC Logs \{#collecting-logs}

```bash
# csi-controller logs
kubectl logs -n <namespace> \
  deployment/hspc-csi-controller \
  -c hspc-csi-driver

# csi-node logs (all nodes)
kubectl logs -n <namespace> \
  daemonset/hspc-csi-node \
  -c hspc-csi-driver
```

:::tip
Set up cluster-level logging to persist logs: [Kubernetes logging documentation](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-administration/logging/)
:::

## Viewing PersistentVolume Properties

To find the LDEV ID and other storage properties of a PV:

```bash
kubectl describe pv <PV-name>
```

Look for `VolumeHandle` and `VolumeAttributes` in the output:

```
VolumeHandle: 01--scsi--900000070010--914--spc-ecdf100f22
VolumeAttributes:
  ldevIDDec=914
  ldevIDHex=03:92
  nickname=spc-ecdf100f22
```

## Simultaneous PVC Load Errors

If you see errors `0x0000100b`, `0x0000100f`, `0x0000101a`, or `0x0000f007` under heavy simultaneous PVC creation load, reduce the `csi-provisioner` worker threads. Edit the HSPC CR and set `--worker-threads` for the `csi-provisioner` container:

```yaml
spec:
  controller:
    containers:
      - name: csi-provisioner
        args:
          - --csi-address=/csi/csi-controller.sock
          - --timeout=300s
          - --v=5
          - --worker-threads=10
          - --default-fstype=ext4
```

The default is 20. A value of 10 is recommended for high-concurrency environments. If the problem persists, contact technical support.

## Host Group Naming Errors (0x00001023)

If error `0x00001023` appears, HSPC cannot find a host group matching its naming rule (`spc-<wwn1>-<wwn2>-<wwn3>`). The error was likely caused by a host group whose name does not follow the naming format.

To resolve:

1. Delete Storage Plug-in for Containers. See [Uninstalling HSPC](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/uninstalling.md#uninstalling-hspc) and [Re-creating HSPC](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/uninstalling.md#re-creating-hspc).
2. Delete the host group specified in the error message.
3. Search for host groups that have WWNs for each host, and delete them or rename them to `spc-<wwn1>-<wwn2>-<wwn3>`.
4. Re-create Storage Plug-in for Containers. See [Re-creating HSPC](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/uninstalling.md#re-creating-hspc).

See [Host Group and iSCSI Target Naming Rules](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/overview.md#host-group-and-iscsi-target-naming-rules) for the full naming convention.

## Notes on Force-Deleting a Pod

Forcibly deleting a Pod (`kubectl delete pod --force --grace-period=0`) while it has an HSPC-managed volume attached can leave the deleted Pod and information about the associated PVC on the node, which may cause unexpected errors.

To properly delete this information, you must restart the relevant node before using the node again.

Before force-deleting:

1. Verify the node where the Pod was running is completely down or the volume is no longer attached.
2. After force-deletion, verify the volume is not still attached to the node in the storage system.

## Creating and Deleting PVCs Simultaneously

Do **not** create and delete PVCs at the same time. Creating a PVC while another is being deleted can result in storage system inconsistencies.

## Host Group Settings

HSPC automatically manages host groups. Do **not** manually modify host groups created by HSPC (`spc-*` prefix). If you need custom host group settings, configure them before installing HSPC or rename existing host groups to match the naming rule. See [Host group naming rules](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/overview.md#host-group-and-iscsi-target-naming-rules).

## Timeout Errors When Creating or Deleting Pods

If Pod creation or deletion times out with HSPC-managed volumes, check:

1. The `csi-controller` log for storage API errors.
2. Storage system connectivity (port 80 or 443).
3. Whether the storage system operation timed out and needs cleanup manually.

If the issue persists, try re-creating the HSPC instance or restarting the affected node.

## Scheduling or Creation Failures

If a Kubernetes pod cannot be scheduled or created:

1. Check node selector and affinity settings.
2. Verify `csi-node` DaemonSet is running on the target node:
   ```bash
   kubectl get daemonset hspc-csi-node -n <namespace>
   ```
3. Check `csi-node` logs for attachment errors.

## Virtual LDEV ID Errors (HSPC0x0000b007)

Virtual LDEV ID errors occur when an invalid or undefined Virtual LDEV ID is associated with a physical LDEV. The error message looks like:

```
Internal desc = [HSPC0x0000b007] a virtual LDEV ID is defined : remove the virtual LDEV ID, and consider using resource partitioning feature if it is not already configured : ldev [140] has a virtual ldev id [140]
```

To resolve using the `raidcom` utility (other tools can also be used):

1. **Verify the LDEV status:**
   ```bash
   raidcom get ldev -ldev_id <ldev-id> -fx
   ```
   Check the `VIR_LDEV` parameter. If the value is `fffe`, it indicates an invalid virtual LDEV entry is mapped.

2. **Remap the virtual LDEV:**
   ```bash
   raidcom map resource -ldev_id <ldev-id> -virtual_ldev_id <ldev-id>
   ```
   Use the same LDEV ID for both parameters. This clears the invalid mapping.

3. **Reverify the LDEV status:**
   ```bash
   raidcom get ldev -ldev_id <ldev-id> -fx
   ```
   Verify using the same LDEV ID. If no virtual LDEV is listed under `VIR_LDEV`, the invalid entry has been successfully removed.

## Advanced Configuration

See [Appendix A: Advanced Configuration](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/appendix.md) for:

- Editing the existing LUN configuration
- Migrating existing ports of a StorageClass
- Modifying bulk mapping mode
- Modifying CPU and memory resources for the CSI Controller deployment
- Creating VM templates with StorageClass clone

:::tip
For recommended configuration practices to prevent common issues — including multipath setup, StorageClass design for provisioning performance, and volume limits — see the [CSI Best Practices Guide](/container-plugins/csi-best-practices/index.md).
:::

***

## Stretched PVC Issues

### Creating a Stretched PVC — Volumes Already Created on Storage System

If Stretched PVC creation does not complete (the status does not change to `Bound`), run the following to check the error details:

```bash
kubectl describe pvc <PVC-name>
```

If the following error message appears, volumes have already been created on the storage system. Deleting the PVC before the status changes to `Bound` will leave those volumes on the storage system without being deleted.

```
failed to provision volume with StorageClass ... : volumes created : [{id:100 serial:11111} {id:200 serial:22222}]
```

After the PVC is deleted, use **Storage Navigator** to delete the volumes and the associated LUNs. For VSP One Block storage systems, use **VSP One Block Administrator** to delete the volumes and the associated LUNs. Then change the virtual LDEV IDs of the deleted volumes to **unassigned (65534)**.

### Stretched PVC — Pair Status Abnormal

If a failure occurs in a storage system, the global-active device pairs configuring the Stretched PVC might have an abnormal status. If an error occurs when using the Stretched PVC, or if a Pod connected to the Stretched PVC becomes unavailable:

1. Refer to the **Global-Active Device User Guide** and perform recovery for the storage system failure and the pair status.
2. If recovery from the failure is difficult, you can create a volume clone from either the primary or secondary storage system and continue using that clone as a regular PVC. See [Cloning from a Stretched PVC](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/storage-classes.md#cloning-from-stretched-pvc).

### Stretched PVC — Host Failure

If a host fails, the status of the Pod running on that host becomes `Terminating` and the Pod enters an abnormal state. To resume the Pod on a healthy host:

1. Forcibly delete the Pod:

```bash
kubectl delete pod <Pod-name> --grace-period=0 --force
```

2. Delete the `VolumeAttachment` related to the Stretched PVC the Pod was using:

```bash
kubectl delete volumeattachment <VolumeAttachment-name>
```



---
url: /container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/appendix.md
---

# Appendix A: Advanced Configuration

## Editing the Existing LUN Configuration

You can modify the LUN configuration for existing PVs if you need to change port assignments or other storage-level settings.

:::warning
Modifying LUN configurations for active PVs can disrupt I/O. Plan maintenance windows accordingly.
:::

1. Identify the LDEV ID from the PV:
   ```bash
   kubectl describe pv <PV-name>
   ```
2. Use Storage Navigator or the REST API to modify the LUN configuration on the storage system.
3. Restart the affected Pods to reconnect with the new configuration.

## Migrating Existing Ports of a StorageClass

To migrate volumes to use different ports (for example, after hardware reconfiguration):

:::note
Port migration is **not supported** for NVMe over FC or NVMe/TCP. Those protocols use NVM subsystem-based addressing.
:::

### Update the StorageClass with the new port IDs
Edit the `portID` parameter in the StorageClass YAML and apply:
```bash
kubectl apply -f sc-updated.yaml
```
:::note
Changes to the StorageClass apply to **new PVCs only** — existing PVCs are not affected.
:::
### For existing PVCs, create new PVCs and migrate data
There is no in-place migration of existing PV port assignments via Kubernetes. To move existing volumes to different ports:
1. Create new PVCs using the updated StorageClass.
2. Copy data from the old PVC to the new PVC.
3. Update application Pod manifests to use the new PVC.
4. Delete the old PVC when migration is complete.

## Configuring the Maximum LUN Count (SPC\_LUN\_MAX)

By default, HSPC supports up to **256 LUNs** per node. You can increase this up to a maximum of **2048** using the `SPC_LUN_MAX` environment variable.

:::warning
Do not set the value for `SPC_LUN_MAX` greater than 2048.
:::

:::tip
For host-level OS configuration required to support more than 256 volumes (kernel arguments, MachineConfig for OpenShift, or DaemonSet for RKE2/vanilla Kubernetes), see [Supporting More Than 256 Volumes per Host](/container-plugins/csi-best-practices/openshift.md#more-than-256-volumes) in the CSI Best Practices Guide.
:::

:::note
Changing the environment value of `SPC_LUN_MAX` also requires appropriate kernel-level configuration based on the HBA driver. Refer to the vendor-specific guide to verify settings.
:::

Scale the operator to 0 replicas before editing to prevent the operator from reverting changes:

```bash
kubectl scale deployment hspc-operator-controller-manager --replicas=0 -n hspc-operator-system
```

Edit the `hspc-csi-node` deployment to add the environment variable:

```yaml title="hspc_v1_hspc.yaml"
spec:
  node:
    containers:
      - name: hspc-csi-driver
        env:
          - name: SPC_LUN_MAX
            value: "512"
```

Scale the operator back up:

```bash
kubectl scale deployment hspc-operator-controller-manager --replicas=1 -n hspc-operator-system
```

## Modifying the Bulk Mapping Mode

Bulk mapping allows HSPC to map multiple LUNs simultaneously, improving performance during provisioning. It is **enabled by default**.

:::note
Bulk mapping is **not supported** on VSP G350, G370, G700, or G900.
:::

To disable bulk mapping, set `IS_BULK_MAPPING_MODE_ENABLED` to `false`:

```yaml title="hspc_v1_hspc.yaml"
spec:
  controller:
    containers:
      - name: hspc-csi-driver
        env:
          - name: IS_BULK_MAPPING_MODE_ENABLED
            value: "false"
```

Scale the operator to 0 before editing and back to 1 after to apply the change cleanly.

## Modifying CPU and Memory Resources for the CSI Controller Deployment

Adjust resource requests and limits for the `hspc-csi-controller` Deployment:

```yaml title="hspc_v1_hspc.yaml"
spec:
  controller:
    containers:
      - name: hspc-csi-driver
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "250m"
            memory: "256Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "1000m"
            memory: "512Mi"
```

Apply the changes by re-creating the HSPC instance.

## CSI Log Collection \{#csi-log-collection}

HSPC includes a log collection tool for simplified support data gathering. The tool collects logs from all HSPC-related pods, cluster context, and storage information into a single archive.

:::tip
On **OpenShift**, the optional [CSI console plugin](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/csi-console-plugin.md#generate-support-bundle) can trigger support bundle generation from the StorageClass UI (Technology Preview). CLI and script-based collection on this page remains the documented product path.
:::

The script is available in the GitHub repository:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/hitachi-vantara/csi-operator-hitachi
cd csi-operator-hitachi/log_collector/hspc/Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle
```

Run the collection script from the CSI Controller node (bash):

```bash
./get_hitachicsilogs.sh
```

A PowerShell variant is also available for Windows environments. See the `README` in the same directory for usage instructions and options.

:::tip
The community-maintained **CSI Log Bundle Viewer** can display the collected logs in a browser-based interface. See [CSI Log Bundle Viewer](/container-plugins/csi-log-bundle-viewer.md).
:::

## Creating VM Templates with StorageClass Clone \{#vm-templates-storageclass-clone}

You can deploy virtual machines from templates using StorageClass clone strategy options. This is supported only on OpenShift Virtualization. Using this method accelerates VM provisioning and ensures that PVCs created for new VMs support volume resizing.

:::note
If the VMs need disk expansion, you must configure host-based cloning. Templates provide a repeatable, maintenance-free way to deploy VMs and ensure DataSource references are correctly set before VM creation.
:::

:::tip
For planning guidance on StorageClass clone strategies, expansion compatibility, and lifecycle considerations, see [VM Templates and StorageClass Clone Strategy](/container-plugins/csi-best-practices/openshift.md#vm-templates) in the CSI Best Practices Guide.
:::

### Set host-based copy cloning on the StorageClass
```bash
oc annotate storageclass <storage-class-name> cdi.kubevirt.io/clone-strategy=copy --overwrite
```
### Create a DataVolume from an existing PVC
Create a file named `datavolume_from_pvc_example.yaml` with the following content, then apply it:
```yaml title="datavolume_from_pvc_example.yaml"
apiVersion: cdi.kubevirt.io/v1beta1
kind: DataVolume
metadata:
  name: rhel9-golden-b28-frompvc
  namespace: openshift-virtualization-os-images
spec:
  source:
    pvc:
      namespace: demo-test
      name: rhel9-vmb28-fromui2-rootdisk
  storage:
    storageClassName: <storage-class-name>
```
```bash
oc apply -f datavolume_from_pvc_example.yaml
```
Monitor the DataVolume and PVC copy status:
```bash
oc get dv -n openshift-virtualization-os-images
```
### Create a DataVolume from a QCOW2 image
Alternatively, create a DataVolume from a QCOW2 image URL:
```yaml title="datavolume_from_qcow2_image_example.yaml"
apiVersion: cdi.kubevirt.io/v1beta1
kind: DataVolume
metadata:
  name: rhel9-golden-jp1
  namespace: openshift-virtualization-os-images
spec:
  source:
    http:
      url: "http://10.x.y.z/images/rhel-9.7-x86_64-kvm.qcow2"
  storage:
    storageClassName: <storage-class-name>
    accessModes:
      - ReadWriteMany
    resources:
      requests:
        storage: 30Gi
```
```bash
oc apply -f datavolume_from_qcow2_image_example.yaml
```
### (Optional) Revert the StorageClass clone strategy annotation
After the DataVolume is created, revert the annotation to the default setting if desired:
```bash
oc annotate storageclass <storage-class-name> cdi.kubevirt.io/clone-strategy- --overwrite
```
### Create a DataSource from the PVC
```yaml title="datasource_example.yaml"
apiVersion: cdi.kubevirt.io/v1beta1
kind: DataSource
metadata:
  name: rhel9-b28-frompvc
  namespace: openshift-virtualization-os-images
spec:
  source:
    pvc:
      name: rhel9-golden-b28-frompvc
      namespace: openshift-virtualization-os-images
```
```bash
oc apply -f datasource_example.yaml
```
### Create a custom VM template
Create `rhel9_template_custom_example.yaml` with the following content. This defines a parameterized RHEL 9 VM template backed by a CDI DataSource, with cloud-init and configurable disk size:
```yaml title="rhel9_template_custom_example.yaml"
apiVersion: template.openshift.io/v1
kind: Template
metadata:
  name: rhel9-from-datasource-b28           # (1) Template name shown in OpenShift catalog
  namespace: openshift
  annotations:
    openshift.io/display-name: "RHEL 9 - HSPC DataSource-56k"
    description: "Creates a virtual machine by cloning root disk from a CDI DataSource
      (image-based provisioning) and configures initial access via cloud-init."
    tags: "rhel9,linux,kubevirt,virtualization"
    iconClass: icon-rhel
    template.openshift.io/bindable: "false"
    template.kubevirt.io/editable: |
      /objects[0].spec.template.spec.domain.cpu.cores
      /objects[0].spec.template.spec.domain.memory.guest
      /objects[0].spec.dataVolumeTemplates[0].spec.storage.resources.requests.storage
      /objects[0].spec.template.spec.volumes
      /objects[0].spec.template.spec.domain.devices.disks
  labels:
    template.kubevirt.io/architecture: amd64
    template.kubevirt.io/type: vm
    workload.template.kubevirt.io/server: "true"
    flavor.template.kubevirt.io/medium: "true"
    name.os.template.kubevirt.io/rhel9: "true"
    app.kubernetes.io/component: templating
    app.kubernetes.io/name: custom-templates
    vm.kubevirt.io/template.namespace: openshift
parameters:
  - name: NAME                              # (2) Parameters are filled in at deploy time
    description: "VirtualMachine name"
    required: true
    value: rhel9-vm
  - name: BOOT_SOURCE_NAME
    description: "CDI DataSource name"
    required: true
    value: rhel9-b28-frompvc               # (3) Must match the DataSource created in step 5
  - name: BOOT_SOURCE_NAMESPACE
    description: "Namespace containing the DataSource"
    required: true
    value: openshift-virtualization-os-images
  - name: STORAGE_CLASS
    description: "StorageClass for the virtual machine root disk (HSPC)"
    required: true
    value: vspone-block28-205-sc           # (4) Replace with your HSPC StorageClass name
  - name: ROOTDISK_SIZE_GI
    description: "Root disk size in Gi"
    required: true
    value: "32"
  - name: CLOUD_USER
    description: "cloud-init username"
    required: true
    value: "cloud-user"
  - name: CLOUD_PASSWORD
    description: "Optional password (leave empty to prefer SSH key)"
    required: false
    value: "redhat123"
  - name: SSH_AUTHORIZED_KEY
    description: "Optional SSH public key for cloud-init"
    required: false
    value: ""
objects:
  - apiVersion: kubevirt.io/v1
    kind: VirtualMachine
    metadata:
      name: ${NAME}
      labels:
        app: ${NAME}
    spec:
      runStrategy: Always
      dataVolumeTemplates:
        - apiVersion: cdi.kubevirt.io/v1beta1
          kind: DataVolume
          metadata:
            name: ${NAME}-rootdisk
            annotations:
              cdi.kubevirt.io/storage.usePopulator: "false"  # (5) Bypasses CDI populators for direct clone
          spec:
            sourceRef:
              kind: DataSource
              name: ${BOOT_SOURCE_NAME}
              namespace: ${BOOT_SOURCE_NAMESPACE}
            storage:
              storageClassName: ${STORAGE_CLASS}
              accessModes: ["ReadWriteMany"]
              volumeMode: Block               # (6) Block mode required for HSPC + OpenShift Virt
              resources:
                requests:
                  storage: "${ROOTDISK_SIZE_GI}Gi"
      template:
        metadata:
          labels:
            kubevirt.io/domain: ${NAME}
            app: ${NAME}
        spec:
          domain:
            firmware:
              bootloader:
                efi:
                  secureBoot: false
            cpu:
              sockets: 1
              cores: 2
            memory:
              guest: 4Gi
            devices:
              disks:
                - name: rootdisk
                  bootOrder: 1
                  disk: { bus: virtio }
                - name: cloudinitdisk
                  disk: { bus: virtio }
              interfaces:
                - name: default
                  model: virtio
                  masquerade: {}
              rng: {}
          networks:
            - name: default
              pod: {}
          volumes:
            - name: rootdisk
              dataVolume:
                name: ${NAME}-rootdisk
            - name: cloudinitdisk
              cloudInitNoCloud:
                userData: |
                  #cloud-config
                  user: ${CLOUD_USER}
                  ssh_pwauth: true
                  chpasswd:
                    expire: false
                    password: ${CLOUD_PASSWORD}
                  ssh_authorized_keys:
                    - ${SSH_AUTHORIZED_KEY}
```
```bash
oc apply -f rhel9_template_custom_example.yaml
```
After applying, the custom template appears in the OpenShift web console under **Template catalog > User templates**.
### Verify the template parameters
```bash
oc process --parameters openshift//rhel9-from-datasource-b28
```
### Create a VM from the template
```bash
oc -n openshift process rhel9-from-datasource-b28-frompvc \
  -p NAME=rhel9-fromb28pvc-2 \
  -p BOOT_SOURCE_NAME=rhel9-b28-frompvc \
  -p BOOT_SOURCE_NAMESPACE=openshift-virtualization-os-images \
  -p STORAGE_CLASS=vspone-block28-205-sc \  # Replace with your StorageClass name
  -p ROOTDISK_SIZE_GI=81 \
  | oc -n demo-test apply -f -              # Replace demo-test with your target namespace
```
### Access the template in the web console
In the OpenShift web console, navigate to **Template catalog > User templates** to find and deploy VMs from your custom RHEL 9 template.

***

## Stretched PVC — Additional Operations

### Checking Information on Stretched PVCs \{#checking-stretched-pvc-info}

After a Stretched PVC is created, a PV is created with storage system resource information stored in `spec.csi.volumeAttributes`. Run the following to inspect:

```bash
kubectl describe pv <PV-name>
```

Check the `VolumeAttributes` section. The following parameters are included:

| Parameter           | Description                                                                 |
| ------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `nickname`          | Nickname of the created LDEV (same for both the primary and secondary site) |
| `primarySerial`     | Serial number of the storage system at the primary site                     |
| `primaryVolumeID`   | ID of the LDEV created at the primary site                                  |
| `secondarySerial`   | Serial number of the storage system at the secondary site                   |
| `secondaryVolumeID` | ID of the LDEV created at the secondary site                                |
| `copyGroupName`     | The copy group name                                                         |
| `copyPairName`      | The copy pair name                                                          |

### Deleting a Stretched PVC

You can delete a Stretched PVC in the same way as deleting a regular PVC. When a Stretched PVC is deleted, the global-active device pair and the volumes of the storage systems at both the primary and secondary sites are also deleted.

:::note
Deleting a Stretched PVC takes more time than deleting a regular PVC. If the Stretched PVC cannot be deleted, follow the guidance in the error message. If the problem persists, see [Forcibly Deleting a Stretched PVC](#forcibly-deleting-a-stretched-pvc) below.
:::

### Forcibly Deleting a Stretched PVC \{#forcibly-deleting-a-stretched-pvc}

To delete a Stretched PVC, the storage systems at both the primary and secondary sites must be running normally. If a failure occurs on either storage system or on the global-active device pair, normal deletion will fail. To force-delete:

1. Check the information about the resources created at both sites. See [Checking Information on Stretched PVCs](#checking-stretched-pvc-info).
2. Delete the PVC and the PV:

```bash
kubectl patch pvc <pvc-name> --type merge -p '{"metadata":{"finalizers":null}}'
kubectl delete pvc <pvc-name>
kubectl delete pv <pv-name>
```

3. Based on the information from step 1, use Storage Navigator to delete the global-active device pair, volumes, and associated LUNs. Then change the virtual LDEV IDs of the deleted volumes to **unassigned (65534)**.



---
url: /container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/release-notes.md
---

# HSPC v3.18.0 Release Notes

**Document:** RN-92ADPTR141-31 (April 2026)

This document provides late-breaking information about Hitachi Storage Plug-in for Containers v3.18.0. It includes information not available when the technical documentation was published, as well as known issues and solutions.

:::note
This is a **major release** that includes new features, performance improvements, and bug fixes.
:::

## About This Release

- **Docs:** [docs.hitachivantara.com](https://docs.hitachivantara.com)
- **Downloads:** [support.hitachivantara.com](https://support.hitachivantara.com)
- **Compatibility:** [compatibility.hitachivantara.com/products/hspc](https://compatibility.hitachivantara.com/products/hspc/)

## Supported Software

| Software                                | Supported Version |
| --------------------------------------- | ----------------- |
| Kubernetes                              | 1.32 – 1.34       |
| Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform    | 4.19 – 4.21       |
| Rancher Kubernetes Engine 2 (RKE2)      | 1.32 – 1.34       |
| Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) | 1.32 – 1.34       |

**Supported Operating Systems:**

| Operating System                 | Version                                   |
| -------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Red Hat Enterprise Linux         | 8.2–8.4, 8.6–8.8, 8.10, 9.0, 9.2–9.4, 9.6 |
| Red Hat Enterprise Linux Core OS | 4.19–4.21                                 |
| Ubuntu                           | 20.04, 22.04, 24.04                       |
| Amazon Linux                     | 2                                         |

:::note
NVMe over Fibre Channel and NVMe/TCP are supported **only** on RHEL 9.x, RHEL Core OS, and Ubuntu.
:::

## Supported Storage Systems

| Storage System                                     | Minimum Firmware                            | Protocol                           |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| VSP One Block High End                             | A0-05-20-00/05                              | Fibre Channel, NVMe/TCP            |
| VSP One B24, B26, B28                              | A3-04-20 or later                           | Fibre Channel, iSCSI, NVMe over FC |
| VSP E1090, E1090H                                  | 93-06-01 or later (NVMe over FC: 93-07-21+) | Fibre Channel, iSCSI, NVMe over FC |
| VSP 5100, 5500, 5100H, 5500H                       | 90-04-04 or later                           | Fibre Channel, iSCSI               |
| VSP 5200, 5600, 5200H, 5600H                       | 90-08-01 or later (NVMe over FC: 90-09-21+) | Fibre Channel, iSCSI, NVMe over FC |
| VSP E590, E790, E990, E590H, E790H                 | 93-02-04 or later                           | Fibre Channel, iSCSI               |
| VSP G350, G370, G700, G900, F350, F370, F700, F900 | 88-06-02 or later                           | Fibre Channel, iSCSI               |
| VSP One SDS Block                                  | 01.08.00.0x or later                        | Fibre Channel, iSCSI, NVMe/TCP     |

## New Features and Enhancements in v3.18.0

- **OpenShift 4.21 support** — Added support for Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform 4.21.
- **Kubernetes 1.34 support** — Added support for Kubernetes 1.34.
- **NVMe/TCP on VSP One Block** — Added NVMe/TCP protocol support for VSP One Block High End and VSP One Block 20 series.
- **GAD pair split and resync** — Added support for split and resync operations on GAD pairs via the new `GadPairAction` custom resource.
- **Multi-resource user for Stretched PVC** — Added multi-resource user support for Stretched PVC configurations.
- **CSI log collection tool** — Added a log collection tool for simplified support data gathering.
- **Telemetry service** — Added an optional telemetry service that reports usage data to Hitachi Vantara.
- **CSI dashboard for RHOCP** _(Tech Preview)_ — Added a dynamic console plugin providing a **HitachiCSI** tab in the OpenShift web console for StorageClass management. See [OpenShift Console Plugin](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/csi-console-plugin.md) in the Quick Reference for installation.

## Fixed Issues

| Issue   | Description                                                    |
| ------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| CPS-80  | No available host group numbers in resource group              |
| CPS-100 | Commvault snapshot backups failing on OpenShift                |
| CPS-150 | Volume expansion fails when snapshot exists on VSP 5000 series |

## Known Limitations

:::warning
Review all limitations before deploying in a production environment.
:::

- **VSP One SDS Block — node addition/removal:** When you add or remove a storage node, Pods created before the change may not function correctly. Restart the plug-in, then restart affected Pods.

- **VSP One SDS Block — NVMe/TCP connections persist:** When removing a storage node, NVMe/TCP connections to the removed node may persist. Restart the node hosting the affected Pod to clear these connections.

- **Stretched PVC primary/secondary swap:** After creating a Stretched PVC using a secret that specifies primary and secondary storage systems, you **cannot** interchange primary and secondary values in that secret.

- **ReadOnlyMany — "read-only file system" error:** Attaching a ReadOnlyMany PVC may result in a "read-only file system" error.
  - **Workaround:** Recreate the Pod with `spec.volumes.persistentVolumeClaim.readOnly: true`.

- **ReadOnlyMany not supported for NVMe over FC.**

- **NVMe over FC — single cluster per NVM subsystem:** Each NVM subsystem must be dedicated to a single Kubernetes cluster. Do **not** share an NVM subsystem across multiple clusters.

- **Multi-AZ not supported** for the cloud model of VSP One SDS Block.

- **OpenShift Virtualization — volume expansion:** After expanding a VM disk volume, the VM may not show the increased size until it is restarted.

- **PVC deletion failure — finalizer:** If PVC deletion fails, manually delete the corresponding PV. If the PV has a finalizer, remove it to allow deletion.

- **ReadOnlyMany is not supported with OpenShift Virtualization.**

- **HTTPS with certificate verification:** The certificate must be installed within the HSPC container image.

- **Stretched PVC pools** can be created only on a virtual storage machine.

- **Mounting a PVC created from a Stretched PVC is not supported.**

## Technology Preview

Technology Preview features provide early access to upcoming innovations for testing only. Not covered by support plans, may not be functionally complete, and not intended for production use.

## Getting Help

- **Support:** [support.hitachivantara.com](https://support.hitachivantara.com/en_us/contact-us.html)
- **Community:** [community.hitachivantara.com](https://community.hitachivantara.com)
- **Doc feedback:** [doc.feedback@hitachivantara.com](mailto:doc.feedback@hitachivantara.com) (include document number RN-92ADPTR141-31 and revision)



---
url: /container-plugins/hrpc/index.md
---

# Hitachi Replication Plug-in for Containers (HRPC)

HRPC extends HSPC with **automated storage replication** between Kubernetes clusters. It exposes Hitachi TrueCopy and Universal Replicator replication operations as Kubernetes Custom Resources, enabling disaster recovery and data protection workflows managed entirely through Kubernetes APIs.

:::note
HRPC requires HSPC to be installed and operational on both the primary and secondary clusters before installation.
:::

## Available Versions

| Version                                                | Status | Release Date  | Doc Number       |
| ------------------------------------------------------ | ------ | ------------- | ---------------- |
| [**3.17.4**](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/index.md) | Latest | February 2026 | MK-92ADPTR155-09 |



---
url: /container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/index.md
---

### [Copy Group Operations](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md)

- [Splitting a Copy Group](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#splitting-a-copy-group)
- [Edit the Replication Group CR from the primary site](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#edit-the-replication-group-cr-from-the-primary-site)
- [Set desiredPairState to split](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#set-desiredpairstate-to-split)
- [Verify the Replication Group CR status is Split](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#verify-the-replication-group-cr-status-is-split)
- [Verify the Replication CR status is Split](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#verify-the-replication-cr-status-is-split)
- [Resynchronizing a Copy Group](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#resynchronizing-a-copy-group)
- [Edit the Replication Group CR from the primary site](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#edit-the-replication-group-cr-from-the-primary-site-1)
- [Set desiredPairState to pair](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#set-desiredpairstate-to-pair)
- [Verify the Replication Group CR status is Pair](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#verify-the-replication-group-cr-status-is-pair)
- [Verify the Replication CR status is Pair](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#verify-the-replication-cr-status-is-pair)
- [Managing Failover of a Copy Group](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#managing-failover-of-a-copy-group)
- [Edit the Replication Group CR from the secondary site](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#edit-the-replication-group-cr-from-the-secondary-site)
- [Set desiredPairState to swap-split](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#set-desiredpairstate-to-swap-split)
- [Verify the Replication Group CR status is Split](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#verify-the-replication-group-cr-status-is-split-1)
- [Verify the Replication CR status is Failover](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#verify-the-replication-cr-status-is-failover)
- [Resynchronizing a Copy Group After Failover (Swap-Resync)](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#resynchronizing-a-copy-group-after-failover-swap-resync)
- [Edit the Replication Group CR from the secondary site](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#edit-the-replication-group-cr-from-the-secondary-site-1)
- [Set desiredPairState to swap-resync](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#set-desiredpairstate-to-swap-resync)
- [Verify the Replication Group CR status is Pair](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#verify-the-replication-group-cr-status-is-pair-1)
- [Verify the Replication CR status is Pair](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#verify-the-replication-cr-status-is-pair-1)
