# Installing and Configuring Replication Plug-in for Containers

This chapter covers pre-installation requirements, supported software, and the full configuration workflow for HRPC.

## Pre-installation Requirements

Before installing HRPC, verify the following requirements are met.

| Component                     | Requirement                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| ----------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Kubernetes cluster            | Different Kubernetes clusters in primary and secondary sites. A single cluster across sites is **not supported**.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| Replication target            | Create a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) using Storage Plug-in for Containers.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| etcd                          | Verify etcd is **not** replicated by HRPC.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| Kubernetes cluster management | Declarative cluster supported. Imperative cluster **not** supported.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| Number of Kubernetes clusters | Primary: one cluster. Secondary: one cluster.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| Number of storage systems     | Primary: one storage system. Secondary: one storage system.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| Replicas per PVC              | One. Do not create any remote copy pair manually with the PVC used for HRPC.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| Pods attached to a PVC        | One per PVC. If a PVC is controlled by HRPC, only one Pod per PVC is allowed.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| Program product               | Universal Replicator and TrueCopy                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| Application redundancy        | Active-Passive. The secondary site is activated only when a failure occurs in the primary site.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| Inter-site connections        | All of the following connections are required: <ul><li>IP connection between HRPC and Kubernetes (both sites must communicate with each other)</li><li>IP connection between HRPC and storage systems (both sites)</li><li>IP connection between primary and secondary storage system REST API servers for management</li><li>FC or iSCSI connection between primary and secondary storage systems for data copy</li></ul> |

## Supported Software Versions

| Software                             | Supported Version                                                                                                                                                     |
| ------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Storage Plug-in for Containers       | v3.17.4                                                                                                                                                               |
| Kubernetes                           | 1.31 – 1.33                                                                                                                                                           |
| Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform | 4.18 – 4.20                                                                                                                                                           |
| Storage systems                      | See [HSPC Release Notes](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.17.4/release-notes.md) and the Universal Replicator / TrueCopy® User Guide for supported storage system versions. |

:::warning
Ops Center API Configuration Manager is **not** supported.
:::

## Configuration Workflow

The following workflow outlines the steps for configuring and using HRPC.

### Configure the storage system
Configure the storage system for replications.
1. Configure the storage system as described in the [HSPC Quick Reference Guide](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.17.4/quick-reference/installation.md).
2. Configure the remote path between primary and secondary site storage systems. See the Universal Replicator User Guide and TrueCopy® User Guide for supported storage systems.
3. Configure journal volumes. See the Universal Replicator User Guide and TrueCopy® User Guide.
### Configure Storage Plug-in for Containers
1. Install HSPC in **both** the primary and secondary sites. See the [HSPC Quick Reference Guide](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.17.4/quick-reference/installation.md).
2. Create `StorageClass` in both sites.
:::note
- The `name` and `fstype` of the `StorageClass` must be the same for both sites.
- The `StorageClass` in the primary site must point to the storage in the primary site.
- The `StorageClass` in the secondary site must point to the storage in the secondary site.
:::
### Create the PersistentVolumeClaim
HRPC replicates a PVC created in this step.
1. Create a PVC with the `StorageClass` created in the previous step. See the [HSPC Quick Reference Guide](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.17.4/quick-reference/provisioning.md).
2. _(Optional)_ Create a Pod that consumes the PVC.
HRPC uses the following PVC parameters when creating the PVC in the secondary site:
- `name`
- `namespace`
- `spec.accessModes`
- `spec.resources`
- `spec.storageClassName`
- `spec.volumeMode`
### Configure Replication Plug-in for Containers
This procedure uses a dedicated management machine that can access both the primary and secondary Kubernetes clusters.
1. Get the `kubeconfig` file from both sites.

2. Set environment variables for the file paths:

**Primary site**

```bash
KUBECONFIG_P=/<directory>/primary-kubeconfig
SECRET_KUBECONFIG_P=/<directory>/primary-kubeconfig-secret.yaml
```


**Secondary site**

```bash
KUBECONFIG_S=/<directory>/secondary-kubeconfig
SECRET_KUBECONFIG_S=/<directory>/secondary-kubeconfig-secret.yaml
```

3. Clone the CSI operator repository and navigate to the HRPC YAML directory:
```bash
git clone https://github.com/hitachi-vantara/csi-operator-hitachi
cd csi-operator-hitachi/hrpc/<HRPC-version>/yaml
```
4. Create the Secret manifest for the **secondary** kubeconfig (used by HRPC running in the primary cluster):
```bash
# Encode the secondary kubeconfig in Base64
cat ${KUBECONFIG_S} | base64 -w 0
```
```yaml title="${SECRET_KUBECONFIG_S}"
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: hspc-replication-operator-remote-kubeconfig
  namespace: hspc-replication-operator-system
type: Opaque
data:
  remote-kubeconfig: <base64-encoded-secondary-kubeconfig>
```
5. Create the Secret manifest for the **primary** kubeconfig (used by HRPC running in the secondary cluster):
```bash
cat ${KUBECONFIG_P} | base64 -w 0
```
```yaml title="${SECRET_KUBECONFIG_P}"
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: hspc-replication-operator-remote-kubeconfig
  namespace: hspc-replication-operator-system
type: Opaque
data:
  remote-kubeconfig: <base64-encoded-primary-kubeconfig>
```
6. Configure storage system credentials in `storage-secrets-sample.yaml`:
```yaml title="storage-secrets-sample.yaml"
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: hspc-replication-operator-storage-secrets
  namespace: hspc-replication-operator-system
type: Opaque
stringData:
  storage-secrets.yaml: |-
    storages:
      - serial: 54321           # (1) Primary storage serial number
        url: https://172.16.1.1 # (2) Primary storage URL
        user: UserPrimary       # (3) Primary storage username
        password: PasswordPrimary # (4) Primary storage password
        journal: 1              # (5) Primary journal ID (Universal Replicator)
      - serial: 400100          # (6) Secondary storage serial number
        url: https://172.16.1.2 # (7) Secondary storage URL
        user: UserSecondary     # (8) Secondary storage username
        password: PasswordSecondary # (9) Secondary storage password
        journal: 2              # (10) Secondary journal ID (Universal Replicator)
```
| Field     | Description                                                                                                                                      |
| --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| (1), (6)  | Storage system serial numbers for primary and secondary sites                                                                                    |
| (2), (7)  | Storage URL. For VSP 5000 series: IP of the SVP. For VSP E/F/G series: IP of the storage controller. For VSP One B20 series: service IP address. |
| (3), (8)  | Username. Must belong to the built-in storage administrator (View & Modify) user group — same as HSPC.                                           |
| (4), (9)  | Password for the username                                                                                                                        |
| (5), (10) | Journal ID for Universal Replicator                                                                                                              |
7. Create namespaces in both sites:
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl create -f hspc-replication-operator-namespace.yaml
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl create -f hspc-replication-operator-namespace.yaml
```
8. Create kubeconfig Secrets in both sites (use **different** manifest files per site):
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl create -f ${SECRET_KUBECONFIG_S}
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl create -f ${SECRET_KUBECONFIG_P}
```
9. Create storage system Secrets in both sites (use the **same** manifest file):
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl create -f storage-secrets-sample.yaml
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl create -f storage-secrets-sample.yaml
```
10. Deploy HRPC in both sites (use the **same** manifest file):
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl create -f hspc-replication-operator.yaml
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl create -f hspc-replication-operator.yaml
```
:::note
If you find any misconfigurations in Secrets, delete the replication controller first, then modify the Secrets. The replication controller reads Secrets only when it starts.
:::
11. Confirm HRPC is running in both sites:
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl get pods -n hspc-replication-operator-system
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl get pods -n hspc-replication-operator-system
```

:::note
If you want to use the resource partitioning feature of HSPC, see [Using the resource partitioning feature of Storage Plug-in for Containers](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/appendix.md#resource-partitioning) before starting this configuration.
:::

## Installing the DR Operator

The Disaster Recovery (DR) Operator automates disaster recovery processes — replication, failover, failback, and resynchronization between primary and secondary sites.

### Prerequisites

- HSPC installed on both clusters (see [HSPC Quick Reference Guide](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.17.4/quick-reference/installation.md))
- HRPC installed on both clusters (see configuration steps above)

### Procedure

### Clone the repository and navigate to the DR operator directory
```bash
git clone https://github.com/hitachi-vantara/csi-operator-hitachi
cd csi-operator-hitachi/hrpc/<version>/dr-operator
```
### Verify required files are present on both sites
| File                           | Location                                                   |
| ------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| `cert-manager.yaml`            | `csi-operator-hitachi/hrpc/<version>/dr-operator/yaml/`    |
| `dr-operator-install.yaml`     | `csi-operator-hitachi/hrpc/<version>/dr-operator/yaml/`    |
| `dr-operator-uninstall.yaml`   | `csi-operator-hitachi/hrpc/<version>/dr-operator/yaml/`    |
| `remote-cluster-management.sh` | `csi-operator-hitachi/hrpc/<version>/dr-operator/scripts/` |
### Install cert-manager on both sites
```bash
kubectl apply -f cert-manager.yaml
```
### Install the DR operator on both sites
```bash
kubectl apply -f dr-operator-install.yaml
```
:::note OpenShift-specific configuration
On Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform, specify the `fsGroup` value for the namespace where the DR operator will be installed.
1. Retrieve the `fsGroup` range for the namespace:
   ```bash
   kubectl get ns <namespace> -o yaml | grep supplemental-groups
   ```
   Example output:
   ```
   openshift.io/sa.scc.supplemental-groups: 1000830000/10000
   ```

2. Update the `fsGroup` value in `dr-operator-install.yaml`:
   ```bash
   vi dr-operator-install.yaml
   ```
   ```yaml
   securityContext:
     fsGroup: 1000830000
     runAsNonRoot: true
   ```
Also update the `storageClassName` parameter in `dr-operator-install.yaml` with the `StorageClass` configured for HRPC.
:::
### Register kubeconfigs between sites
```bash
# Register secondary kubeconfig at primary site
./remote-cluster-management.sh <secondary-site> secondary-kubeconfig

# Register primary kubeconfig at secondary site
./remote-cluster-management.sh <primary-site> primary-kubeconfig
```
### Verify cert-manager is running
```bash
kubectl get pods -n cert-manager
```
Expected output:
```
NAME                                       READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
cert-manager-786fb48656-xtrmr             1/1     Running   0          9h
cert-manager-cainjector-5965d694d9-m6nnj  1/1     Running   0          9h
cert-manager-webhook-5bf5f9c8f6-zmtdf     1/1     Running   0          9h
```
### Verify the HRPC namespace exists
```bash
kubectl get ns
```
The `hspc-replication-operator-system` namespace should be present and `Active`.
### Verify the DR operator controller pod is running
```bash
kubectl get pod -n hspc-replication-operator-system
```
Expected output:
```
NAME                                                   READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
hspc-dr-operator-controller-manager-dbbc5546d-snl7d   1/1     Running   82         3d9h
```
### Verify the remote-kubeconfig Secret
```bash
kubectl get secret -n hspc-replication-operator-system
kubectl describe secret remote-kubeconfig -n hspc-replication-operator-system
```
### Verify the DRPolicy CRD is installed
```bash
kubectl get crd | grep drpolicies
```
Expected output:
```
drpolicies.hspc.hitachi.com   2025-10-22T17:06:12Z
```



---
url: /container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/default-copy-groups.md
---

# Replicating Storage Volumes Using Default Copy Groups

This chapter describes how to replicate storage volumes by creating a Replication Custom Resource (CR). HRPC manages the process by replicating the specified PVC and initiating creation of a corresponding PVC at the secondary site. Data is protected using Universal Replicator or TrueCopy.

You can also replicate storage volumes using a DR policy. See [Managing DR Operations with the DR Operator](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/dr-operator.md).

:::note

- All replications on a storage system must belong to the same resource group. If multiple resource groups or resource partitioning are used, ensure new replications are assigned to the same resource group as existing ones.
- To create a replication in a different resource group, first delete the existing replication, then create a new one.

For details on the resource partitioning feature, see [Using the resource partitioning feature of HSPC](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/appendix.md#resource-partitioning).
:::

## Replication Types

| Type                          | Mode         | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| ----------------------------- | ------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **TrueCopy (TC)**             | Synchronous  | Data written to the primary volume (P-VOL) is simultaneously written to the secondary volume (S-VOL). Host I/O is acknowledged only after both volumes receive the data. Ensures data consistency but introduces latency.             |
| **Universal Replicator (UR)** | Asynchronous | Data written to the P-VOL is first stored in a master journal volume, then transferred to a restore journal volume on the remote system. Host I/O is acknowledged immediately without waiting for the data to reach the remote S-VOL. |

## Creating a Replication CR

### Specify the target PVC
Set `persistentVolumeClaimName` to the PVC created in [Creating the PersistentVolumeClaim](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/installation.md#configure-storage-plug-in-for-containers). This PVC must exist before creating the Replication CR.
### Specify the StorageClass
Set `storageClassName` to the `StorageClass` name used when creating the PVC.
### Set the replication type
Set `replicationType` to `TC` for TrueCopy or `UR` for Universal Replicator.
### Set the fence level (TrueCopy only)
For TrueCopy, `fenceLevel` is **mandatory**. Set it to `DATA`, `NEVER`, or `STATUS`. See the TrueCopy® User Guide for details.
### Create the manifest file
```yaml title="hspc_v1_replication.yaml"
apiVersion: hspc.hitachi.com/v1
kind: Replication
metadata:
  name: replication-sample
spec:
  persistentVolumeClaimName: pvc-sample     # (1) Target PVC name
  storageClassName: sc-sample               # (2) StorageClass name
  replicationType: TC                       # (3) TC or UR
  fenceLevel: DATA                          # (4) DATA, NEVER, or STATUS (TC only)
```
:::warning
After creating a Replication CR, you **cannot edit** any parameters except `desiredPairState`. To change parameters, delete the existing Replication CR and recreate it.
:::

## Creating the Replication CR Object

Create the Replication CR object from the primary site. This triggers creation of a TrueCopy or Universal Replicator pair and an initial data copy.

```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl create -f hspc_v1_replication.yaml
```

:::note

- When HRPC detects a newly created Replication CR, it automatically creates a corresponding Replication CR and PVC in the secondary site.
- The site where the Replication CR is created is treated as the primary site.
- The Replication CR creates a copy pair in a **default copy group**. Subsequent pairs with similar manifests are added to the same default copy group.

Default copy group names:

- Universal Replicator: `spc<PrimaryStorageDeviceID><SecondaryStorageDeviceID>`
- TrueCopy: `spc<PrimaryStorageDeviceID><SecondaryStorageDeviceID>t`

Default copy pair name: `spc-<PrimaryVolumeLDEVID>-<SecondaryVolumeLDEVID>`
:::

For details on the CR creation sequence, see [Details on TrueCopy and Universal Replicator pair creation](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/appendix.md#pair-creation-details).

## Verifying Replication CR Status

The Replication CR status changes to `Pair` when initial replication is created and data protection starts. Transition to `Pair` depends on data size and may take some time.

```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl get replication
```

```
NAME                STATUS   DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
replication-sample  Pair     none           none        12m
```

```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl get replication
```

```
NAME                STATUS   DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
replication-sample  Pair     none           none        12m
```

If you made an error creating the Replication CR, delete it and check whether a PVC with the specified name exists in the secondary site:

```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl delete -f hspc_v1_replication.yaml
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl delete pvc <PVC-name>
```



---
url: /container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-pair-operations.md
---

# Performing Replication Operations on Copy Pairs

This chapter describes replication operations for individual replication copy pairs. These operations are available after the Replication CR is created and in `Pair` status.

Before performing these operations, verify that the Replication CR was created using the **default copy group** or a **custom copy group not managed by a parent replication group**. For copy group-level operations, see [Copy Group Operations](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md).

:::note
Perform replication operations from a **single remote site only** (for example, the primary site). Do not modify the Replication CR at the other remote site during the operation.
:::

## Splitting a Pair

Splitting stops data copy from primary to secondary site. The secondary volume becomes independently accessible.

### Confirm the Replication CR is in Pair status
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl get replication
```
Verify `STATUS` is `Pair` and `OPERATION` is `none`.
### Edit the Replication CR
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl edit replication <Replication-name>
```
### Set desiredPairState to split
```yaml
spec:
  desiredPairState: split
```
### Confirm the status is Split
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl get replication
```
```
NAME                STATUS   DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
replication-sample  Split    split          none        6d20h
```

## Resynchronizing a Pair

Resynchronizing resumes data copy from primary to secondary site.

:::note
You **cannot** perform the resync operation if a Pod mounts the secondary volume in the secondary site.
:::

### Confirm the Replication CR is in Split status
Verify `STATUS` is `Split` and `OPERATION` is `none`.
### Edit the Replication CR
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl edit replication <Replication-name>
```
### Set desiredPairState to pair
```yaml
spec:
  desiredPairState: pair
```
### Confirm the status is Pair
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl get replication
```
```
NAME                STATUS   DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
replication-sample  Pair     pair           none        6d20h
```

## Managing Failover

In a planned failover, you run applications from the secondary site. This stops data copy from primary to secondary and allows writes to the secondary volume.

:::note
Do **not** modify the replication in the primary site during this operation.
:::

### Verify Replication CR is in Pair status
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl get replication
```
Verify `STATUS` is `Pair` and `OPERATION` is `none`.
### Edit the Replication CR from the secondary site
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl edit replication <Replication-name>
```
### Set desiredPairState to failover
```yaml
spec:
  desiredPairState: failover
```
### Confirm failover status
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl get replication
```
```
NAME                STATUS    DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
replication-sample  Failover  failover       none        6d20h
```
:::note
- The Replication CR status at the **primary site** is `Split`.
- The Replication CR status at the **secondary site** is `Failover`.
- Applications can now write data to the secondary volume (S-Vol).
:::

## Resynchronizing After Failover (Swap-Resync)

After a planned failover, with applications running from the secondary site, data can be synchronized back to the primary volume. This operation swaps the replication direction — the secondary site acts as the new primary.

:::note
After a swap-resync, the direction of replication (data copy and journal) is **reversed** from secondary to primary.
:::

### Verify the Replication CR is in Failover status
Confirm `STATUS` is `failover` and `OPERATION` is `none`.
### Edit the Replication CR from the secondary cluster
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl edit replication <Replication-name>
```
### Set desiredPairState to swap-resync
```yaml
spec:
  desiredPairState: swap-resync
```
### Verify the status is Pair
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl get replication
```
```
NAME               STATUS   DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
sample-replication Pair     swap-resync    none        19m
```

## Verifying Replicated Data

From the secondary site, you can deploy a Pod and mount the secondary volume to confirm the replicated data.

**Before you begin:** Verify the Replication CR status is `Split` in the primary site, or `Failover`/`Split` in the secondary site.

1. Create a Pod that uses the PVC of the secondary site to check the data.
2. Delete the Pod after the data check is complete.



---
url: /container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/custom-copy-groups.md
---

# Replicating Storage Volumes Using Custom Copy Groups

A **Copy Group** is a logical container that manages and organizes replication pairs (P-VOL and S-VOL) between primary and secondary storage systems. It allows administrators to perform replication operations collectively on multiple volume pairs, ensuring consistency and simplifying management.

This chapter describes how to replicate storage volumes using user-defined custom copy groups in the Replication CR.

## Benefits of Custom Copy Groups

- **Consistency management:** Copy groups can be associated with a user-defined CTG-ID.
- **Journal management:** Copy groups can be linked to a Journal-ID separate from storage secrets.
- **Replication control:** Copy groups can be included in Replication Groups, enabling a single replication operation to be applied collectively across multiple replication pairs.

## Creating a Replication Group CR

A Replication Group CR represents a one-to-one copy group relationship. Replication operations performed using the Replication Group CR apply to all replication pairs within the copy group managed by the group.

:::note

- Replication Group CR must be created at the **primary site**. Once the Replication CR for the custom copy group is created and reaches `Pair` state, the Replication Group CR is automatically triggered at the secondary site.
- Hitachi recommends that you do **not** perform replication pair-level operations if the pair is part of a copy group managed by a parent Replication Group. To manage pairs independently, set `desiredPairState` to `none` in the Replication Group CR at both sites.
- You **cannot** add or move an existing replication pair across a copy group. A pair can only be associated with a copy group during creation.
- You **cannot** use an existing copy group name not created by HRPC.
- Hitachi recommends using the same replication type (UR or TC) and the same consistency group ID for all pairs in the same copy group. For Universal Replicator, use the same journal ID.

:::

### Create the Replication Group CR manifest
```yaml title="hspc_v1_replicationgroup.yaml"
apiVersion: hspc.hitachi.com/v1
kind: ReplicationGroup
metadata:
  name: <parent-replication-group-name>
  namespace: <namespace>
spec:
  desiredPairState: none
```
### Create the Replication Group CR object
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl create -f hspc_v1_replicationgroup.yaml
```

## Creating a Replication CR for a Custom Copy Group

### Create the Replication CR manifest with custom copy group parameters
```yaml title="hspc_v1_replication.yaml"
apiVersion: hspc.hitachi.com/v1
kind: Replication
metadata:
  name: <custom-replication-name>
  namespace: <namespace>
spec:
  persistentVolumeClaimName: <PVC-name>         # (1)
  storageClassName: <StorageClass-name>          # (2)
  copyGroupName: spc-<copy-group-name>           # (3) Optional
  copyPairName: spc-<copy-pair-name>             # (4) Optional if copyGroupName set
  replicationType: TC                            # (5) TC or UR
  fenceLevel: NEVER                              # (6) NEVER, DATA, or STATUS
  consistencyGroupId: 0                          # (7) Optional, 0–255
  pvolJournalId: 1                               # (8) Required for UR
  svolJournalId: 2                               # (9) Required for UR
  parentName: <ReplicationGroup-CR-name>         # (10) Optional
```
| Field | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| ----- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| (1)   | Target PVC protected by TrueCopy or Universal Replicator                                                                                                                                                                   |
| (2)   | StorageClass name from which the target PVC was created                                                                                                                                                                    |
| (3)   | _(Optional)_ Unique, case-sensitive name, 1–29 characters, **must start with `spc-`** prefix. Omitting the prefix will cause an error.                                                                                     |
| (4)   | _(Optional when copyGroupName set)_ Unique, case-sensitive name, 1–31 characters, **must start with `spc-`** prefix. Omitting the prefix will cause an error. Cannot be set without `copyGroupName`.                       |
| (5)   | `TC` for TrueCopy pair or `UR` for Universal Replicator pair                                                                                                                                                               |
| (6)   | `NEVER`, `DATA`, or `STATUS`. Mandatory for TrueCopy; optional for Universal Replicator.                                                                                                                                   |
| (7)   | _(Optional)_ Consistency Group ID, 0–255. For VSP E590/E790: 0–127. Cannot be set without `copyGroupName`. You cannot have multiple consistency group IDs for the same `copyGroupName`. Assign a unique ID per copy group. |
| (8)   | P-Vol Journal ID. Required for UR when `copyGroupName` is set; optional for TC. Assign unique IDs per copy group.                                                                                                          |
| (9)   | S-Vol Journal ID. Required for UR when `copyGroupName` is set; optional for TC. Assign unique IDs per copy group.                                                                                                          |
| (10)  | _(Optional)_ Name of the Replication Group CR that manages operations for this copy group                                                                                                                                  |
:::note
When multiple Replication CRs are created with the **same copy group name** but different copy pair names, all copy pairs are grouped under the same copy group.
:::
### Create the Replication CR object
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl create -f hspc_v1_replication.yaml
```



---
url: /container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md
---

# Performing Replication Operations on Copy Groups

After a Replication CR for a custom copy group is in `Pair` state, you can perform copy group-level operations by modifying the Replication Group CRs.

When `parentName` is **not** specified in the Replication CR, operations are performed at the copy pair level. See [Copy Pair Operations](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-pair-operations.md).

## Splitting a Copy Group

Splits all TrueCopy or Universal Replicator pairs in the copy group. Data copy from primary to secondary is stopped during the split.

**Before you begin:**

- Verify `desiredState` of all Replication CRs is `none` at both sites.
- Verify `desiredState` of all Replication Group CRs is `none` at both sites.

### Edit the Replication Group CR from the primary site
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl edit replicationgroup <replication-group-name>
```
### Set desiredPairState to split
```yaml
spec:
  desiredPairState: split
```
### Verify the Replication Group CR status is Split
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl get replicationgroup
```
```
NAME                    STATUS   DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
replicationgroup-sample Split    split          none        6d20h
```
:::note
The replication pair status for all copy groups managed by the replication group will be in the Split state.
:::
### Verify the Replication CR status is Split
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl get replication
```
```
NAME                STATUS   DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
replication-sample  Split    none           none        6d20h
```

## Resynchronizing a Copy Group

Resumes data copy from primary to secondary for all pairs in the copy group.

**Before you begin:**

- Verify `desiredState` of all Replication CRs is `none` at both sites.
- Verify `desiredState` of all Replication Group CRs is `none` at the secondary site.

### Edit the Replication Group CR from the primary site
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl edit replicationgroup <replication-group-name>
```
### Set desiredPairState to pair
```yaml
spec:
  desiredPairState: pair
```
### Verify the Replication Group CR status is Pair
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl get replicationgroup
```
```
NAME                    STATUS   DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
replicationgroup-sample Pair     pair           none        6d20h
```
### Verify the Replication CR status is Pair
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl get replication
```
```
NAME                STATUS   DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
replication-sample  Pair     pair           none        6d20h
```

## Managing Failover of a Copy Group

Performs a `swap-split` on all pairs in the copy group, enabling applications to run from the secondary site.

**Before you begin:**

- Verify `desiredState` of all Replication CRs is `none` at both sites.
- Verify `desiredState` of all Replication Group CRs is `none` at the **primary** site.

### Edit the Replication Group CR from the secondary site
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl edit replicationgroup <replication-group-name>
```
### Set desiredPairState to swap-split
```yaml
spec:
  desiredPairState: swap-split
```
### Verify the Replication Group CR status is Split
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl get replicationgroup
```
```
NAME                    STATUS   DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
replicationgroup-sample Split    swap-split     none        17m
```
### Verify the Replication CR status is Failover
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl get replication
```
```
NAME                STATUS    DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
replication-sample  Failover  none           none        6d20h
```
:::note
- Replication CR status at the primary site is `Split`.
- Replication CR status at the secondary site is `Failover`.
- Applications can now write data to the secondary volume (S-Vol).
:::

## Resynchronizing a Copy Group After Failover (Swap-Resync)

Resumes data copy from secondary to primary and enables the secondary site to act as the new primary.

:::note
After this operation, the direction of replication (data copy and journal) is **reversed** from secondary to primary.
:::

**Before you begin:**

- Verify `desiredState` of all Replication CRs is `none` at both sites.
- Verify `desiredState` of all Replication Group CRs is `none` at the primary site and `swap-split` at the secondary site.

### Edit the Replication Group CR from the secondary site
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl edit replicationgroup <replication-group-name>
```
### Set desiredPairState to swap-resync
```yaml
spec:
  desiredPairState: swap-resync
```
### Verify the Replication Group CR status is Pair
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl get replicationgroup
```
```
NAME                    STATUS   DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
replicationgroup-sample Pair     swap-resync    none        19m
```
### Verify the Replication CR status is Pair
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl get replication
```
```
NAME                STATUS   DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
replication-sample  Pair     none           none        6d20h
```



---
url: /container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/expanding-volumes.md
---

# Expanding Volumes

To expand the capacity of a Replication pair volume, expand the secondary site first, then the primary site.

## Before You Begin

- The difference management method of the storage system must be **hierarchical difference management**.

  For details, see the Universal Replicator User Guide or TrueCopy® User Guide. This setting must be applied to storage systems at both primary and secondary sites.

:::warning
The system option mode setting for hierarchical difference management affects **all** remote copy pairs in the storage system. Review the documentation for your storage system before changing this setting.
:::

- If expanding a Replication volume created **before** changing to hierarchical difference management, first perform a [split](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-pair-operations.md#splitting-a-pair) then a [resync](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-pair-operations.md#resynchronizing-a-pair) before proceeding.

## Procedure

### Split the replication pair
See [Splitting a TrueCopy pair or Universal Replicator pair](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-pair-operations.md#splitting-a-pair).
### Expand the volume in the secondary site
See the [HSPC Quick Reference Guide](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.17.4/quick-reference/expanding-volumes.md) for volume expansion procedures.
:::note
After expansion, the capacity of the volumes must be the **same** in both primary and secondary sites.
:::
### Expand the volume in the primary site
See the [HSPC Quick Reference Guide](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.17.4/quick-reference/expanding-volumes.md) for volume expansion procedures.
:::note
After expansion, the capacity of the volumes must be the **same** in both primary and secondary sites.
:::
### Resynchronize the replication pair
See [Resynchronizing a TrueCopy pair or Universal Replicator pair](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-pair-operations.md#resynchronizing-a-pair).



---
url: /container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/failover-failback.md
---

# Performing Failover and Failback

This chapter outlines procedures for failover and failback — moving applications from primary to secondary site during a disaster or maintenance event, and returning them to the primary site once recovery is complete.

:::note
These procedures involve running composite replication operations in sequence — specifically, **failover** (or swap-split) followed by **swap-resync**.
:::

## Running Applications at the Secondary Site

Use this procedure for a planned switchover or when the primary site is unavailable.

### Stop applications at the primary site (planned switchover only)
For unplanned failures, skip this step.
### Perform failover from the secondary site
See [Managing failover for TrueCopy or Universal Replicator pairs](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-pair-operations.md#managing-failover).
### Perform failover of the replication group (if configured)
See [Managing failover of a TrueCopy or Universal Replicator copy group](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#managing-failover-of-a-copy-group).
### Prepare and deploy applications at the secondary site
Create application manifests using the PVCs created by HRPC at the secondary site. Deploy the applications and verify they are running correctly.
### After primary site recovery — copy data back to primary
a. Resynchronize the replication pair from the secondary site:
See [Resynchronizing after failover](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-pair-operations.md#resynchronizing-after-failover-swap-resync).
b. If you configured a replication group, resynchronize from the secondary site:
See [Resynchronizing a copy group after failover](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#resynchronizing-a-copy-group-after-failover-swap-resync).

## Performing Failback to the Primary Site

When the primary site is available or planned maintenance is complete, restore applications to the primary site.

**Before you begin:**

- Verify the Replication CR or Replication Group CR exists at the primary site.
- Verify applications are **shut down** at the secondary site before failing back.
- Verify all data written at the secondary site has been copied and resynchronized to the primary site.

### Stop applications at the secondary site
### Perform failover from the primary site
See [Managing failover for TrueCopy or Universal Replicator pairs](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-pair-operations.md#managing-failover).
### Perform failover of the replication group from the primary site (if configured)
See [Managing failover of a TrueCopy or Universal Replicator copy group](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#managing-failover-of-a-copy-group).
### Deploy applications at the primary site
Create manifests using PVCs at the primary site, deploy, and verify they are running correctly.
### Copy data from primary to secondary
a. Resynchronize the replication pair from the primary site:
See [Resynchronizing after failover](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-pair-operations.md#resynchronizing-after-failover-swap-resync).
b. Resynchronize the replication group from the primary site:
See [Resynchronizing a copy group after failover](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/replication/copy-group-operations.md#resynchronizing-a-copy-group-after-failover-swap-resync).

## Running Without Primary Site Recovery

If you do not want to fail back to the primary site after a failover, see [Deleting replication CRs without connection to a remote site](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/deleting-resources.md#deleting-without-remote-connection) to delete Replication CRs and the TrueCopy or Universal Replicator pairs.



---
url: /container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/dr-operator.md
---

# Managing Disaster Recovery with the DR Operator

The Disaster Recovery (DR) Operator is a Kubernetes-native disaster recovery orchestrator that automates and simplifies DR operations between primary and secondary storage systems. It manages replication workflows — split, failover, failback, and resynchronization for TrueCopy and Universal Replicator pairs — ensuring minimal downtime and consistent data protection across sites.

## DR Operation Flow

![DR operation flow diagram showing three phases: Normal Operations (primary active, TC/UR replication to secondary standby), Failover (primary unavailable, secondary cluster activates and secondary storage promoted to primary role), and Failback (primary recovered, resync reverses data back, original roles restored)](/images/hrpc/dr-operation-flow.svg)
## DR Operator Capabilities

- **Eliminates manual, per-PVC operations** — Operate at the application or namespace level.
- **Scalable DR** — Manage large Kubernetes environments with many volumes.
- **User-defined policies** — Define DR policies at the application, namespace, or PVC level.
- **Application-aware DR** — Perform DR based on defined policies with ordered startup.

:::note
Deploying the DR Operator on a **hub cluster** is not supported. Deploy on both primary and secondary clusters.
:::

For DR Operator installation, see [Installing the DR Operator](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/installation.md#installing-the-dr-operator).

## Creating and Applying a DR Policy

A DR policy can be defined for an application, a namespace, or specific PVCs.

### Create a DR Policy manifest on the primary site
```yaml title="sample-app-drpolicy.yaml"
apiVersion: hspc.hitachi.com/v1alpha2
kind: DRPolicy
metadata:
  name: sample-app-drpolicy
spec:
  pvcSelector:
    # matchWorkloadLabels:
    #   - app: sample_app
    matchNamespaces:
      - demo-db
      - demo-app
    # matchPvcs:
    #   - demo-db-pvc
    #   - demo-server-pvc
    #   - demo-ui-pvc
  drTarget:
    clusterName: site-62        # (1) Secondary site name
    replicationMode: sync       # (2) sync (TrueCopy) or async (Universal Replicator)
    consistencyRequired: true   # (3) true to enable consistency group
    consistencyGroupName: Name  # (4) Required when consistencyRequired is true
    #primaryJournalId: 12       # (5) Primary journal ID (Universal Replicator only)
    #secondaryJournalId: 2      # (6) Secondary journal ID (Universal Replicator only)
    #desiredState: pair         # Default is pair
  readyOrderOfPvcByNamespace:
    - demo-db
    - demo-app
  actionsOnTarget:
    - scriptType: shell
      configMapRef:
        name: post-failover-script
        key: run.sh
```
| Field | Description                                                     |
| ----- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| (1)   | Secondary site name where replication must be performed         |
| (2)   | `sync` for TrueCopy pair; `async` for Universal Replicator pair |
| (3)   | `true` to support consistency group                             |
| (4)   | Required when `consistencyRequired` is `true`                   |
| (5)   | Primary journal ID (Universal Replicator only)                  |
| (6)   | Secondary journal ID (Universal Replicator only)                |
:::note
- For Universal Replicator pairs, `replicationMode` must be `async`.
- `desiredState` defaults to `pair`.
- If creating a DR policy for namespaces, create the same namespaces in the secondary site.
- If creating for PVCs, create the corresponding namespaces in the secondary site.
:::
### Apply the DR policy
```bash
kubectl apply -f sample-app-drpolicy.yaml
```
:::note
The DR policy is applied on the secondary site by default.
:::
### Verify the DR policy is applied on both sites
```bash
kubectl get drpolicy
```
```
NAME                 SOURCE_CLUSTER  TARGET_CLUSTER  REPLICATION_TYPE  DESIRED_STATE  STATUS   CTG_ID  AGE
sample-app-drpolicy  site-61         site-62         sync              pair           Healthy  21      6m9s
```
### Verify the replication status of the PVCs
```bash
kubectl get replication -A
```
```
NAMESPACE   NAME                                              STATUS  DESIREDSTATE  OPERATION  AGE
namespace1  replication--namespace1-test--pvc-name1          Pair    pair          none       1m
namespace2  replication--namespace2-test--pvc-name2          Pair    pair          none       1m
```

## Splitting Using the DR Operator

Temporarily stops data replication, allowing independent access to the secondary volume for backup, testing, or recovery.

**Before you begin:** Verify the DR policy is created (see above).

```bash
kubectl patch drpolicy <drpolicy-cr-name> --type=merge -p '{"spec": {"desiredState":"split"}}'
```

Verify the DR policy is applied on both sites:

```bash
kubectl get drpolicy
```

```
NAME                 SOURCE_CLUSTER  TARGET_CLUSTER  REPLICATION_TYPE  DESIRED_STATE  STATUS     CTG_ID  AGE
sample-app-drpolicy  site-61         site-62         sync              split          Unhealthy  21      18m
```

Verify the replication status of the PVCs:

```bash
kubectl get replication -A
```

```
NAMESPACE  NAME                                                    STATUS  DESIREDSTATE  OPERATION  AGE
wp-app     replication--my-app-drpolicy--wp-content-pvc           Split   split         none       20m
wp-db      replication--my-app-drpolicy--mysql-data-mysql-0       Split   split         none       20m
wp-db      replication--my-app-drpolicy--mysql-logs-mysql-0       Split   split         none       20m
```

## Failover Using the DR Operator

Promotes the secondary volume to primary when the original primary site becomes unavailable.

**Before you begin:** Verify the failover is running from the **secondary site**.

```bash
kubectl patch drpolicy <drpolicy-cr-name> --type=merge -p '{"spec": {"desiredState":"failover"}}'
```

Verify the DR policy is applied on both sites:

```bash
kubectl get drpolicy
```

```
NAME                 SOURCE_CLUSTER  TARGET_CLUSTER  REPLICATION_TYPE  DESIRED_STATE  STATUS     CTG_ID  AGE
sample-app-drpolicy  site-61         site-62         sync              failover       Unhealthy  21      20m
```

Verify the replication status of the PVCs:

```bash
kubectl get replication -A
```

```
NAMESPACE  NAME                                               STATUS    DESIREDSTATE  OPERATION  AGE
wp-app     replication--my-app-drpolicy--wp-content-pvc      Failover  failover      none       139m
wp-db      replication--my-app-drpolicy--mysql-data-mysql-0  Failover  failover      none       139m
wp-db      replication--my-app-drpolicy--mysql-logs-mysql-0  Failover  failover      none       140m
```

:::note

- After failover completes, VMs power on and deployment pods scale up at the secondary site.
- If the namespace range for an application differs between primary and secondary sites, pods may not scale up automatically and an error message will be displayed. Update the `fsGroup` value to the namespace range of the secondary site.

:::

## Swap-Resync Using the DR Operator

Reverses primary and secondary roles and restores data synchronization after a failover.

:::note
This operation is **not supported** for Universal Replicator pairs when multiple replication pairs are created using the same Journal ID.
:::

**Before you begin:**

- Verify VMs are powered off.
- Verify the secondary site is up and running.

```bash
kubectl patch drpolicy <drpolicy-cr-name> --type=merge -p '{"spec": {"desiredState":"swap-resync"}}'
```

Verify the DR policy is applied on both sites:

```bash
kubectl get drpolicy
```

```
NAME                 SOURCE_CLUSTER  TARGET_CLUSTER  REPLICATION_TYPE  DESIRED_STATE  STATUS     CTG_ID  AGE
sample-app-drpolicy  site-61         site-62         sync              swap-resync    Unhealthy  21      25m
```

Verify the replication status of the PVCs:

```bash
kubectl get replication -A
```

```
NAMESPACE  NAME                                                        STATUS  DESIREDSTATE  OPERATION    AGE
demo-app   replication--apps-1-drpolicy-sync--demo-server-pvc         Pair    swap-resync   swap-resync  25m
demo-app   replication--apps-1-drpolicy-sync--demo-ui-pvc             Pair    swap-resync   swap-resync  25m
demo-db    replication--apps-1-drpolicy-sync--demo-db-pvc             Pair    swap-resync   swap-resync  25m
```

You must also verify the replication status on the primary site.

## Failback Using the DR Operator

Restores the original replication direction after a failover, making the recovered site the secondary and reestablishing normal data synchronization.

Perform on the **primary site**:

```bash
kubectl patch drpolicy <drpolicy-cr-name> --type=merge -p '{"spec": {"desiredState":"failback"}}'
```

Verify the DR policy is applied on both sites:

```bash
kubectl get drpolicy
```

```
NAME                 SOURCE_CLUSTER  TARGET_CLUSTER  REPLICATION_TYPE  DESIRED_STATE  STATUS   CTG_ID  AGE
sample-app-drpolicy  site-61         site-62         sync              failback       Healthy  21      35m
```

Verify the replication status of the PVCs:

```bash
kubectl get replication -A
```

```
NAMESPACE  NAME                                                        STATUS  DESIREDSTATE  OPERATION  AGE
demo-app   replication--apps-1-drpolicy-sync--demo-server-pvc         Pair    failover      none       42m
demo-app   replication--apps-1-drpolicy-sync--demo-ui-pvc             Pair    failover      none       42m
demo-db    replication--apps-1-drpolicy-sync--demo-db-pvc             Pair    failover      none       42m
```

You must also verify the replication status on the secondary site.

## Deleting a DR Policy CR

When you delete a DR Policy CR, all associated Replication CRs are also deleted.

```bash
kubectl delete drpolicy <dr-policy-cr-name>
```

Verify deletion:

```bash
kubectl get drpolicy
# No resources found in default namespace.

kubectl get replication -n <namespace-of-the-pvc>
# No resources found.
```

## Uninstalling the DR Operator

Uninstall from **both** primary and secondary sites:

```bash
kubectl delete -f dr-operator-uninstall.yaml --ignore-not-found=true
```



---
url: /container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/deleting-resources.md
---

# Deleting Replication and Replication Group CRs

## Deleting Replication CRs (Normal)

Deleting Replication CRs requires access to Kubernetes clusters and storage systems at both primary and secondary sites.

:::note
If your remote site is not accessible, see [Deleting without remote connection](#deleting-without-remote-connection) below.
:::

### Delete the Replication Group CR from the primary site
```bash
kubectl delete -f hspc_v1_replicationgroup.yaml
```
:::note
This also deletes the Replication CR managed by the Replication Group at both sites.
:::
### Delete the Replication CR from the primary site
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl delete -f hspc_v1_replication.yaml
```
This also deletes the Replication CR from the secondary site.
### Verify the Replication CR is deleted from both sites
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl get replication
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl get replication
```
### Verify the Replication Group CR is deleted from both sites
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl get replicationgroup
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl get replicationgroup
```
:::note
If the Replication Group is not deleted at the secondary site and all managed Replication CRs have been deleted at both sites, remove the finalizers of the Replication Group at the secondary site to complete deletion.
:::
### Delete remaining PVCs from both sites
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl delete pod <Pod-name>
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl delete pvc <PVC-name>
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl delete pod <Pod-name>
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl delete pvc <PVC-name>
```

## Deleting Without Remote Connection \{#deleting-without-remote-connection}

If the remote site is not accessible but you must delete Replication CRs, you can force deletion.

### Enable force deletion
```bash
kubectl set env -n hspc-replication-operator-system \
  deployments/hspc-replication-operator-controller-manager \
  SPC_REPLICATION_ALLOW_FORCE_DELETION=true
```
### Delete the Replication CRs
```bash
kubectl delete -f hspc_v1_replication.yaml
```
### Clean up storage assets manually
Using Storage Navigator, delete the Universal Replicator pair corresponding to the Replication CR — it remains after force deletion and must be removed manually.
### Reset the force deletion flag
```bash
kubectl set env -n hspc-replication-operator-system \
  deployments/hspc-replication-operator-controller-manager \
  SPC_REPLICATION_ALLOW_FORCE_DELETION=false
```



---
url: /container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/uninstalling.md
---

# Uninstalling Replication Plug-in for Containers

### Delete all Replication Group CRs from the primary site
```bash
kubectl delete -n <namespace> replicationgroup <replication-group-name>
```
### Delete all Replication CRs from the primary site
```bash
kubectl delete -n <namespace> replication <Replication-name>
```
### Verify all Replication CRs are deleted from both sites
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl get replication
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl get replication
```
### Verify all Replication Group CRs are deleted from both sites
```bash
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl get replicationgroup
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl get replicationgroup
```
### Delete all HRPC resources
Delete all resources created during [Configuring Replication Plug-in for Containers](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/installation.md#configure-replication-plug-in-for-containers).
### Uninstall Storage Plug-in for Containers
See the [HSPC Quick Reference Guide — Uninstalling HSPC](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.17.4/quick-reference/uninstalling.md).



---
url: /container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/troubleshooting.md
---

# Troubleshooting

:::note
If you cannot create or delete PVCs or Pods using HSPC, see the [Troubleshooting section in the HSPC Quick Reference Guide](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.17.4/quick-reference/troubleshooting.md).
:::

## Troubleshooting Replication CR Issues

Review [Details on TrueCopy and Universal Replicator pair creation](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/appendix.md#pair-creation-details) to understand how Replication CRs are created before troubleshooting.

Creating a Replication CR triggers HRPC to create a `LocalVolume` and a `RemoteVolume` CR. These CRs gather information about primary and secondary site storage volumes.

**When the Replication CR status does not change to an expected status:**

```bash
kubectl describe -n <namespace> <CRD-type> <CR-name>
```

There are three CRD types: `Replication`, `LocalVolume`, and `RemoteVolume`. The name of these resources is the same as the Replication in the primary site.

If an error message appears in the newest event log, follow the instructions in the error message to fix the problem. Also check the event logs for `LocalVolume` and `RemoteVolume` CRs in both sites.

:::note
Operations performed on a Replication Group CR or Replication CR may not immediately reflect the status. The status is updated only after reconciliation is completed among sites and custom resources, which can take **5–10 minutes**. Monitor status with:

```bash
kubectl get replication --watch
kubectl get replicationgroup --watch
```

:::

## Collecting Logs

### HRPC Logs

```bash
kubectl logs -n hspc-replication-operator-system \
  deploy/hspc-replication-operator-controller-manager
```

:::tip
Set up cluster-level logging to persist logs: [kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-administration/logging](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-administration/logging/)
:::

:::tip
To collect HSPC and HRPC-related logs, cluster context, and **both clusters** in one bundle (with a browser viewer and DR Policy views when applicable), you can use the community [Hitachi CSI Log Bundle](/container-plugins/csi-log-bundle.md) scripts with primary and secondary kubeconfigs. It is not a Hitachi Vantara product.
:::

### Recent Events for a CR

```bash
kubectl describe -n <namespace> <CRD-type> <CR-name>
```

## Collecting Storage System Information

- **If using an SVP:** Collect the regular dump files.
- **If not using an SVP:** Collect system dumps using the maintenance utility. See the HPE XP8 Remote Web Console User Guide.

## Information Needed When Contacting Support

| Information           | How to Collect                                                                                                                                                                    |
| --------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cluster information   | `kubectl cluster-info dump -A > dump.txt`                                                                                                                                         |
| HRPC logs             | See [Collecting HRPC Logs](#hrpc-logs) above                                                                                                                                      |
| HRPC manifests        | Get the YAML files for HRPC                                                                                                                                                       |
| Replication manifests | Get the YAML files for Replication                                                                                                                                                |
| PVC-related manifests | Get the YAML files for StorageClass, Secret, and PVC                                                                                                                              |
| HSPC logs             | See the [HSPC Quick Reference Guide](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.17.4/quick-reference/troubleshooting.md); optional bundle: [CSI Log Bundle](/container-plugins/csi-log-bundle.md) |
| Application manifests | Get YAML files for applications using volumes created by HSPC                                                                                                                     |
| Storage logs          | See [Collecting Storage System Information](#collecting-storage-system-information) above                                                                                         |

**Support contact:** [support.hitachivantara.com](https://support.hitachivantara.com/en_us/contact-us.html)

## Restarting HRPC

When you make configuration changes, restart HRPC:

```bash title="Restart at primary site"
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_P} kubectl rollout restart deployment \
  -n hspc-replication-operator-system \
  hspc-replication-operator-controller-manager
```

```bash title="Restart at secondary site"
KUBECONFIG=${KUBECONFIG_S} kubectl rollout restart deployment \
  -n hspc-replication-operator-system \
  hspc-replication-operator-controller-manager
```



---
url: /container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/appendix.md
---

# Appendix

## Replication CR Status Reference \{#cr-status-reference}

The following table describes all possible statuses of a Replication CR.

| Status | Description | Pair State |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Pending | Accepted CR creation command. | N/A |
| WaitingPrimarySite | Waiting for primary site to finish TrueCopy or Universal Replicator operations. Applies to secondary site only. | N/A |
| Copying | Initial copy is in progress. | COPY |
| Pair | Successfully created TrueCopy pair or Universal Replicator pair and the state is "PAIR". | PAIR |
| Split | Successfully split TrueCopy pair or Universal Replicator pair and the state is "PSUS" or "SSUS". | PSUS or SSUS |
| Failover / Swap-split | Failover successful for a TrueCopy pair or Universal Replicator pair. | SSWS |
| Swap-resync | Successfully swapped P-Vol and S-Vol. | PAIR |
| Error | Errors in TrueCopy pair or Universal Replicator pair. | PSUE, PFUL, or PFUS |
| Unknown | A pair does not exist or unexpected pair state. | Other |


## Storage Information of Replication CR

Information about the Universal Replicator pair is stored in the Replication CR under `.status.replicationPair`. Retrieve it with:

```bash
kubectl describe -n <namespace> replication <replication-name>
```

| Key                       | Description                                  |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| `localSerialNumber`       | Serial number of the local site storage      |
| `localStorageDeviceId`    | Storage device ID of the local site storage  |
| `remoteSerialNumber`      | Serial number of the remote site storage     |
| `remoteStorageDeviceId`   | Storage device ID of the remote site storage |
| `primaryVolumeId`         | Volume ID of the primary volume              |
| `primaryVolumeNickname`   | Volume nickname of the primary volume        |
| `secondaryVolumeId`       | Volume ID of the secondary volume            |
| `secondaryVolumeNickname` | Volume nickname of the secondary volume      |

:::note
"Local" and "remote" depend on which site you ran `kubectl describe` from.
:::

## Details on TrueCopy and Universal Replicator Pair Creation \{#pair-creation-details}

When you run `kubectl create -f hspc_v1_replication.yaml`, the following CRs are created in order:

| Order | Site      | CRD            | Description                                                                                         |
| ----- | --------- | -------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| 1     | Primary   | `LocalVolume`  | A UR or TC P-Vol created by users. Becomes `true` when the specified PVC exists.                    |
| 2     | Secondary | `LocalVolume`  | A UR or TC S-Vol created by HRPC. Becomes `true` when PVC is successfully created.                  |
| 3     | Secondary | `RemoteVolume` | A UR or TC P-Vol reference. Becomes `true` when HRPC confirms the PVC exists in the primary site.   |
| 4     | Primary   | `RemoteVolume` | A UR or TC S-Vol reference. Becomes `true` when HRPC confirms the PVC exists in the secondary site. |
| 5     | Both      | `Replication`  | Becomes `Pair` when HRPC confirms UR or TC pair creation and initial data copy.                     |

When all CRs are successfully created:

```bash
kubectl get localvolume
```

```
NAME                READY   AGE
replication-sample  true    6d20h
```

```bash
kubectl get remotevolume
```

```
NAME                READY   AGE
replication-sample  true    6d20h
```

```bash
kubectl get replication
```

```
NAME                STATUS   DESIREDSTATE   OPERATION   AGE
replication-sample  Pair     none           none        6d20h
```

## Timezone Settings

HRPC supports the following environment variables:

| Variable                 | Description                                                    |
| ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `TZ`                     | Timezone used for logging. Default is `UTC`.                   |
| `SPC_VERIFY_CERTIFICATE` | If `true`, HRPC verifies TLS certificates. Default is `false`. |

## Using the Resource Partitioning Feature of HSPC \{#resource-partitioning}

If you want to use the resource partitioning feature of HSPC, configure the following in **both** primary and secondary sites. Configuring resource partitioning in only one site is not supported. See the [HSPC Quick Reference Guide](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.17.4/quick-reference/configuration.md) for more information.

| Item                           | Description                                                                                 |
| ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Storage Plug-in for Containers | Enable resource partitioning                                                                |
| Journal                        | Create a journal from the resource group configured in HSPC                                 |
| Host Group                     | Allocate one undefined host group ID for each port in the resource group configured in HSPC |
| Storage system user            | Use the same storage system user configured in HSPC                                         |

:::note
A host group named `spc-replication` will be created when you create a Replication. If a host group with the same name but a different resource group already exists on the same port, **delete the existing host group** before proceeding.
:::

## Recovering and Cleaning Up the Primary Site

:::warning
Reset your Kubernetes cluster in the primary site **before** connecting to the network. If Replication CRs remain in the cluster, an unexpected TrueCopy or Universal Replicator pair might be created automatically and could overwrite data being consumed in the secondary site.
:::

### Initialize the Kubernetes cluster in the primary site (before network connection)
### Deploy HRPC in the primary site
See [Configuring Replication Plug-in for Containers](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/installation.md#configure-replication-plug-in-for-containers).
### Configure HRPC in the secondary site with the new primary kubeconfig
See [Configuring Replication Plug-in for Containers](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/installation.md#configure-replication-plug-in-for-containers).
### Restart HRPC
See [Restarting HRPC](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/troubleshooting.md#restarting-hrpc).
### Identify and delete primary site storage assets
Check storage assets in the primary site to identify the volume ID. See [Storage Information of Replication CR](#storage-information-of-replication-cr). Then use Storage Navigator to delete the identified assets from the primary site.

## Performing Failback After Cleaning Up the Primary Site

**Before you begin:** Complete [Recovering and Cleaning Up the Primary Site](#recovering-and-cleaning-up-the-primary-site).

### Create the Replication CR in the secondary site
### Copy data from secondary to primary
From the primary site:
1. Confirm the Replication CR status is `Pair` and operation is `none`.
2. Edit the Replication CR and change `spec.desiredPairState` to `failover`.
3. Confirm the Replication CR status is `Failover` and operation is `none`.
### Delete the Replication CR and PVC from the secondary site
### Create the Replication CR in the primary site



---
url: /container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/release-notes.md
---

# HRPC v3.17.4 Release Notes

**Document:** RN-92ADPTR153-10 (March 2026)

This document provides late-breaking information about Hitachi Replication Plug-in for Containers v3.17.4. It includes information not available at the time the technical documentation was published, as well as known issues and solutions.

:::note
This is a **major release** that includes enhancements. The version number has been updated to 3.17.x to maintain consistency with Hitachi Storage Plug-in for Containers versioning.
:::

## About This Release

For the most current documentation and downloads, visit:

- **Docs:** [docs.hitachivantara.com](https://docs.hitachivantara.com)
- **Downloads:** [support.hitachivantara.com](https://support.hitachivantara.com)
- **Compatibility:** [compatibility.hitachivantara.com/products/hrpc](https://compatibility.hitachivantara.com/products/hrpc/)

## Supported Software

| Software                               | Supported Version |
| -------------------------------------- | ----------------- |
| Hitachi Storage Plug-in for Containers | v3.17.4           |
| Kubernetes                             | 1.31 – 1.33       |
| Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform   | 4.18 – 4.20       |

**Supported Operating Systems:**

- Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8.2–8.4, 8.6–8.8, 8.10, 9.0, 9.2–9.4, 9.6
- Red Hat Enterprise Linux Core OS 4.18–4.20

## Supported Storage Systems

| Storage System                                     | Minimum Firmware Version |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------ |
| VSP One Block B85                                  | A0-05-20-00/05 or later  |
| VSP One B24, B26, B28                              | A3-04-02 or later        |
| VSP 5100, 5500, 5100H, 5500H                       | 90-04-04 or later        |
| VSP 5200, 5600, 5200H, 5600H                       | 90-08-01 or later        |
| VSP E1090, E1090H                                  | 93-06-01 or later        |
| VSP E590, E790, E990, E590H, E790H                 | 93-02-04 or later        |
| VSP G350, G370, G700, G900, F350, F370, F700, F900 | 88-06-02 or later        |

## Enhancements in v3.17.4

- **Default Replication Status:** Changed from `Ready` to `Pair`.
- **Replication CR Enhancements:** Added `Last Error Message` and `Operation Phase` parameters under the Current Operation section.
- **DR Policy CR Enhancements:** Added `Pod/VM/Deployment`, `Replication Name`, and `Replication Status` parameters under the Selected PVCs section.
- **Failover Behavior:** Virtual machines or deployment pods now automatically power on or scale up at the secondary site after a failover completes.
- **Codebase Updates:** Minor improvements to the HRPC/DR Operator codebase.

## Restrictions and Considerations

:::warning
Review all restrictions before deploying in a production environment.
:::

- TrueCopy replication pairs created with the default copy group are registered in a consistency group **only** when the `isConsistencyGroup` parameter is set to `true`.
- Do **not** use the same `consistencyGroupId` or journal volumes across multiple copy groups (including both default and custom copy groups).
- The Replication Group CR at the secondary site is **not automatically deleted**. Remove it manually by clearing the finalizers.
- Replication operations may not immediately update status. Reconciliation across sites can take **5–10 minutes**. Monitor with `kubectl get replication --watch` or check logs.
- The `desiredState` of a Replication Group is visible **only at the site where the operation is performed**.
- Managing multiple copy groups under a single parent Replication Group can significantly increase the time required for replication operations.
- The `replicationMode` field in the DR Policy CR is **immutable** — it cannot be modified after creation.
- **NVMe over Fibre Channel is not supported.**
- **ReadOnlyMany is not supported.**
- Do **not** perform failover when there is a size mismatch between the primary and secondary volumes.
- HRPC integration with OpenShift Virtualization is supported with the following limitations:
  - Limited automated failback. See [Performing Failback to the Primary Site](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/failover-failback.md#performing-failback-to-the-primary-site).
  - Kubernetes-native snapshot/clone of a PV protected by HRPC is **not supported**. You can manually create snapshots or clones from the backend VSP storage.
- When using HTTPS with certificate verification enabled, the certificate must be installed into the HRPC container image.

## Technology Preview

Technology Preview features provide early access to upcoming Hitachi Vantara innovations for testing purposes. They are **not covered by support plans**, may not be functionally complete, and are **not intended for production use**.

Hitachi Vantara may create bug reports on behalf of support cases. These reports will be forwarded to Engineering as proposed inclusions in future releases.

## Getting Help

- **Support:** [support.hitachivantara.com](https://support.hitachivantara.com/en_us/contact-us.html)
- **Community:** [community.hitachivantara.com](https://community.hitachivantara.com)
- **Doc feedback:** [doc.feedback@hitachivantara.com](mailto:doc.feedback@hitachivantara.com) (include document number RN-92ADPTR153-10 and revision level)



---
url: /container-plugins/hspp/index.md
---

# Hitachi Storage Plug-in for Prometheus (HSPP)

HSPP is a **Prometheus exporter for Hitachi VSP storage systems**. It collects storage capacity, IOPS, transfer rate, and response time metrics and pushes them to Prometheus every 5 minutes, enabling unified monitoring of both Kubernetes resources and underlying VSP storage in Grafana.

HSPP also correlates storage metrics with Kubernetes pod and PV data (sourced from HSPC), enabling pod-level storage visibility.

:::note
HSPP requires HSPC to be installed first. It reads Kubernetes PV metadata created by HSPC to provide pod-level metric correlation.
:::

## Available Versions

| Version                                                | Status | Release Date  | Doc Number       |
| ------------------------------------------------------ | ------ | ------------- | ---------------- |
| [**3.17.4**](/container-plugins/hspp/v3.17.4/index.md) | Latest | February 2026 | MK-92ADPTR156-07 |



---
url: /container-plugins/hspp/v3.17.4/index.md
---
