url: /container-plugins/csi-best-practices/index.md
---

# Hitachi CSI Best Practices Guide

**Document:** MK-SL-445-00 (April 2026) — Initial release

This guide describes best practices for configuring and managing Hitachi Virtual Storage Platform (VSP) storage systems and Hitachi Container Storage Interface (CSI) in Red Hat OpenShift and other Kubernetes environments.

:::note
This guide applies to the hardware and software listed below. For the latest compatibility information, see the [Hitachi Product Compatibility Guide](https://compatibility.hitachivantara.com/products/hspc).
:::

## Scope

### Supported Storage Platforms

| Hardware                                     | Models                                       |
| -------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Hitachi Virtual Storage Platform One Block   | Mid-range (B24, B26, B28) and High End       |
| Hitachi Virtual Storage Platform 5000 series | VSP 5100, 5200, 5500, 5600 (and H variants)  |
| Hitachi Virtual Storage Platform E series    | VSP E590, E790, E990, E1090 (and H variants) |

### Hitachi CSI Software Components

| Component                                                         | Description                                                                                               |
| ----------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Hitachi Storage Plug-in for Containers (HSPC)                     | Main CSI driver and operator — manages volume provisioning, attachment, snapshots, cloning, and expansion |
| Hitachi Replication Plug-in for Containers (HRPC) and DR Operator | Asynchronous and synchronous replication between clusters; policy-based failover and failback             |
| Hitachi Storage Plug-in for Prometheus (HSPP)                     | Exposes storage metrics to Prometheus, visualized through Grafana                                         |

### Hitachi Storage Software Features

The following Hitachi SVOS RF features are referenced in this guide:

- Dynamic Provisioning (HDP)
- Dynamic Tiering (DT)
- Thin Image (TI) / Thin Image Advanced (TIA)
- ShadowImage (SI)
- TrueCopy (TC)
- Universal Replicator (UR)
- Global-active device (GAD)

## How to Use This Guide

| Topic                                                                                                     | See                                                                                    |
| --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| OpenShift and Kubernetes deployment best practices, VM migration, provisioning performance, volume limits | [OpenShift Best Practices](/container-plugins/csi-best-practices/openshift.md)         |
| Disaster recovery with HRPC DR Operator, stretched clusters and Stretch PVC                               | [Disaster Recovery](/container-plugins/csi-best-practices/disaster-recovery.md)        |
| HSPC installation and configuration reference                                                             | [HSPC Quick Reference Guide](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/index.md) |
| Supported versions and compatibility matrix                                                               | [Compatibility Matrix](/container-plugins/compatibility.md)                            |

## About Hitachi CSI

Hitachi CSI (Hitachi Container Storage Interface) refers to the full suite of CSI plug-ins and associated components that integrate Hitachi storage platforms with Kubernetes distributions such as Red Hat OpenShift, SUSE Rancher, and other container orchestration environments.

The supported storage platforms include VSP, VSP One Block, and VSP One SDS Cloud in Azure, AWS, and GCP public clouds.

The CSI enables platform operators to dynamically or statically provision and manage volumes from reliable, high-performance Hitachi storage systems for use by virtual machines (VMs) and container workloads. Key capabilities include:

- Snapshots, cloning, and expansion
- Raw block mode
- Access modes: RWX, RWO, RWOP
- Protocol support: Fibre Channel, iSCSI, NVMe over FC, NVMe/TCP
- Standard PVCs, replicated PVCs for multi-site, and stretched PVCs for metro HA
- Monitoring via HSPP (Prometheus/Grafana)



---
url: /container-plugins/csi-best-practices/openshift.md
---

# Hitachi CSI and Red Hat OpenShift Best Practices

## Red Hat OpenShift

Red Hat OpenShift is an application platform that leverages Kubernetes to streamline developing, deploying, and managing applications whether on public-cloud, on-premises, hybrid-cloud, or edge architecture. With HSPC, Hitachi storage is seamlessly integrated with OpenShift to provide persistent storage whether applications are deployed on public-cloud, on-premises, or hybrid-cloud environments.

### Prerequisites

Before installing HSPC, configure your storage system:

- In VSP One Block Administrator, ensure the storage pool is configured for Hitachi Dynamic Provisioning (HDP), and the storage port is configured with:
  - **Connection Type:** P-to-P (Point-to-Point)
  - **Fabric:** ON
  - **Security:** Enabled

### Setting Up Multipath on Worker Nodes

Configure multipath on worker nodes to ensure high availability.

**OpenShift (recommended):**

```bash
# Clone the repository to obtain the multipath MachineConfig sample
git clone https://github.com/hitachi-vantara/csi-operator-hitachi
```

```bash
oc apply -f multipath-machineconfig-sample.yaml
```

For stretched cluster configurations with GAD, apply the appropriate `multipath.conf` using MachineConfig. See [How to Configure Device Mapper Multipath on Red Hat and SUSE Linux](https://docs.hitachivantara.com) for details.

**SUSE environments:** See the [SUSE Rancher + Hitachi storage guide](https://documentation.suse.com/trd/hitachi/html/gs_virtualization_hitachi-storage/index.html).

### Installing HSPC on OpenShift

Deploy HSPC using the Operator from OperatorHub in the OpenShift web console. See the [HSPC Installation Guide](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/installation.md) for detailed steps.

### First-Time Volume Attachment

After installing HSPC and deploying a stateful container or VM for the first time, volume attachment may fail. Understanding the provisioning flow helps prevent this:

1. When a PVC/PV is created, the associated volume is created on VSP One Block. Host groups are **not** created on the storage side yet.
2. When a container or VM is deployed with the PVC, HSPC automatically creates host groups on the associated ports.
3. When volumes are provisioned to a **new** host/worker node, some environments require a host reboot to recognize newly provisioned volumes. After this first-time reboot, new volumes are recognized automatically.
4. If you reboot worker nodes after the first volume attachment, Kubernetes/HSPC may evacuate the container/VM to other worker nodes, and the newly rebooted nodes might not recognize HSPC volumes.

**Recommended:** Manually provision a small temporary volume after HSPC creates the host group, then reboot the host to verify the data path (FC zoning or iSCSI) is correctly configured.

***

## Red Hat OpenShift Virtualization

### PVC Volume Mode

For OpenShift Virtualization, HSPC supports **RWX** access mode with the **Block** volume mode only.

### Virtual Machine Creation with Small Volumes (vTPM and UEFI) \{#vtpm-uefi}

In OpenShift Virtualization environments backed by Hitachi CSI, VM creation may fail when vTPM or UEFI state persistence is enabled. These features require the provisioner to create small persistent volumes for state data.

**Issue:** VSP storage systems enforce a minimum volume size of **42 MB**, but OpenShift Virtualization provisions TPM and UEFI state volumes smaller than this limit by default.

**Resolution:** Edit the `StorageProfile` for your StorageClass to specify the minimum supported PVC size of `42Mi`. The example below shows a StorageProfile for a VSP B28-backed StorageClass named `storageclass-hitachi-vsp-b85`:

```yaml title="storageprofile-patch.yaml"
apiVersion: cdi.kubevirt.io/v1beta1
kind: StorageProfile
metadata:
  annotations:
    cdi.kubevirt.io/minimumSupportedPvcSize: 42Mi  # (1) Minimum 42 MB to match VSP floor
  labels:
    app: containerized-data-importer
    app.kubernetes.io/component: storage
    app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: cdi-controller
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: hyperconverged-cluster
    app.kubernetes.io/version: 4.19.12              # (2) Match your OpenShift CDI version
    cdi.kubevirt.io: ""
  name: storageclass-hitachi-vsp-b85                # (3) Replace with your StorageClass name
…
```

For more details, see the [Red Hat article on minimum PVC size](https://access.redhat.com/articles/7120382) and the [KubeVirt persistent TPM/UEFI documentation](https://kubevirt.io/user-guide/compute/persistent_tpm_and_uefi_state/).

***

## Migration Toolkit for Virtualization (MTV)

Migrating workloads from VMware to OpenShift Virtualization requires a robust automated approach. The Migration Toolkit for Virtualization (MTV) Operator provides comprehensive tools for VM migration with cold and warm migration strategies.

### Migration Types

**Cold Migration:**

- The VM is powered off during migration.
- MTV copies the entire VM disk and configuration while offline.
- Higher downtime — the VM is unavailable for the entire migration duration.

**Warm Migration:**

- The VM remains powered on during most of the migration, with a short cutover (\~2–3 minutes).
- MTV copies VM disks while running, then performs a final short sync at cutover.
- Disks are transferred in parallel by different pods, making it optimal for large amounts of data distributed across multiple disks.

:::note
Live migration is available only for migrations **between OpenShift Virtualization clusters** or between namespaces on the same cluster.
:::

### Accelerating VM Migration with Storage Offload

Hitachi has partnered with Red Hat to provide a storage offload feature in MTV, available as **Technology Preview starting with MTV 2.9** and **generally available for cold migration in MTV 2.11**.

If both your VMware vSphere cluster and OpenShift Virtualization environment are backed by the same VSP One storage system, storage offload significantly streamlines migration:

| Benefit                             | Detail                                                                             |
| ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Up to 10× faster migration          | Internal testing shows up to 90% reduction in migration time (10 hours → \~1 hour) |
| No IP network dependency            | VM volume data stays on the VSP storage system                                     |
| Reduced compute host resource usage | Less CPU and memory spent on migration tasks                                       |

:::note
As of MTV 2.11, storage offload for **cold migration** is generally available. Storage offload for **warm migration** remains Technology Preview. Check the [MTV release notes](https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/migration_toolkit_for_virtualization/) for current status.
:::

For more information:

- [Hitachi blog: Replatform Faster with OpenShift + VSP One Storage Offload](https://www.hitachivantara.com/en-us/blog/replatform-faster-openshift-vsp-one-storage-offload)
- [MTV documentation](https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/migration_toolkit_for_virtualization/)
- [VM migration options with VSP storage systems](https://docs.hitachivantara.com/v/u/en-us/application-optimized-solutions/mk-sl-410)
- [Configuring MTV with storage offload](https://docs.hitachivantara.com/v/u/en-us/hitachi-integrated-systems/mk-sl-374)

**Installation:** HSPC must be installed to use MTV storage offload.

### Migration Considerations

When migrating VMs with multiple disks:

- **Cold Migration:** Disks are transferred sequentially per VM. Best for VMs with large data on a single disk.
- **Warm Migration:** Disks are transferred in parallel. Best for VMs with data distributed across multiple disks and minimal downtime requirements.

**Operational considerations:**

- Prepare a dedicated network for VM migration, including iSCSI connections. (For MTV storage offload with Fibre Channel, this may not apply.)
- Monitor storage processor resource utilization closely during migration.
- Limit concurrent migrations using the `controller_max_vm_inflight` parameter or the **Max concurrent virtual machine migrations** setting in the GUI (default: up to 20 disks).

***

## Migrating Stateful Containers Across StorageClasses or Clusters

Migrating stateful containers across StorageClasses or between Kubernetes clusters is fully supported using the **Migration Toolkit for Containers (MTC)**. MTC orchestrates migration of both application resources and persistent data.

Hitachi has validated that the HSPC CSI driver integrates with MTC, enabling reliable PVC migration across different StorageClasses or Kubernetes clusters with minimal disruption and preserved data integrity.

:::tip
If you need to update storage port settings in a StorageClass, see [Migrating Existing Ports of a StorageClass](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/appendix.md#migrating-existing-ports-of-a-storageclass) in the HSPC Appendix.
:::

***

## VM Templates and StorageClass Clone Strategy \{#vm-templates}

When deploying VMs from templates in OpenShift Virtualization or upstream KubeVirt, the storage backend determines how PVCs are created. Many CSI drivers support fast-clone mechanisms, which significantly accelerate VM provisioning.

However, the selected clone strategy affects whether resulting PVCs support volume expansion. Plan the cloning approach based on VM lifecycle requirements.

:::warning
If VMs created from templates are expected to require disk expansion in the future, configure the StorageClass to use a **host-based copy clone strategy**. This ensures that PVCs created for new VMs fully support volume resizing.
:::

```bash
oc annotate storageclass <storage-class-name> cdi.kubevirt.io/clone-strategy=copy --overwrite
```

After creating the template, verify its parameters and deploy a VM:

```bash
# Check available template parameters
oc process --parameters openshift//rhel9-from-datasource-b28

# Create a VM from the template
oc -n openshift process rhel9-from-datasource-b28-frompvc \
  -p NAME=rhel9-fromb28pvc-2 \
  -p BOOT_SOURCE_NAME=rhel9-b28-frompvc \
  -p BOOT_SOURCE_NAMESPACE=openshift-virtualization-os-images \
  -p STORAGE_CLASS=vspone-block28-205-sc \   # Replace with your StorageClass name
  -p ROOTDISK_SIZE_GI=81 \
  | oc -n demo-test apply -f -               # Replace demo-test with your target namespace
```

:::tip
For the complete step-by-step procedure — DataVolume creation, DataSource setup, and the full parameterized Template YAML — see [Creating VM Templates with StorageClass Clone](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/appendix.md#vm-templates-storageclass-clone) in the HSPC Appendix.
:::

***

## Improving Volume Provisioning Performance at Scale \{#provisioning-at-scale}

In large Kubernetes clusters connected to VSP One Block storage through many ports, volume provisioning times may increase as the environment scales. The number of ports per StorageClass is a key factor.

### Why Multiple Ports Per StorageClass Creates a Bottleneck

In the HSPC CSI driver, StorageClasses define explicit port lists that control volume-to-port mapping. When a StorageClass contains **12 or more ports**, each newly provisioned volume must be mapped to every port listed — increasing API calls and attachment latency.

**Internal testing (70 pods and PVCs, 12 total ports):**

| Configuration                   | Relative Performance |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------- |
| 1 StorageClass × 12 ports       | Baseline             |
| 3 StorageClasses × 4 ports each | **3× faster**        |
| 6 StorageClasses × 2 ports each | **4× faster**        |

The volume size itself is not a contributing factor.

### Recommended: Use Multiple StorageClasses with Fewer Ports

Instead of a single StorageClass with many ports, use multiple StorageClasses with **6 or fewer ports each**:

```
Instead of:   SC-1 → 12 ports

Use:          SC-1 → 4 ports
              SC-2 → 4 ports
              SC-3 → 4 ports
```

### Distributing Volume Provisioning with MutatingAdmissionPolicy

To automatically rotate StorageClass selection across multiple StorageClasses, use a **MutatingAdmissionPolicy (MAP)**. See the [Kubernetes MutatingAdmissionPolicy documentation](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/access-authn-authz/mutating-admission-policy/).

:::note
As of the time of this guide's release, MutatingAdmissionPolicy is in **Technology Preview** for Red Hat OpenShift. Verify current support status before deployment.
:::

**Example: RKE2 environment with three StorageClasses and MAP**

:::note
The source document presented this example as screenshots. The YAML below is reconstructed from the described structure, standard RKE2/Kubernetes API conventions, and the named StorageClasses (`vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-1/2/3`) referenced in the text. The logic and resource types are authoritative; adjust field values to match your environment.
:::

### Enable the Feature Gate in RKE2
Add two `kube-apiserver-arg` entries to `/etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml` — one to enable the internal logic and one to expose the API group so `kubectl` can see the resource:
```yaml title="/etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml"
token: my-shared-secret
tls-san:
  - rke2-cl2-ub-master1.rke2cl2-ub.scelab.local
kube-apiserver-arg:
  # 1. Enable the internal logic (the "Feature Gate")
  - "feature-gates=MutatingAdmissionPolicy=true"
  # 2. Enable the API group so kubectl can see the resource
  - "runtime-config=admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1beta1=true"
```
Restart the RKE2 service, then verify the API group is available:
```bash
systemctl restart rke2-server

# Verify the MutatingAdmissionPolicy API is registered
kubectl api-resources | grep mutatingadmissionpolicy
```
### Create multiple StorageClasses
Create three StorageClasses, each with 4 iSCSI ports and a shared HSPC Secret. The port sets are divided so no single StorageClass maps to more than 4 ports:
```yaml title="vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-1.yaml"
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-1  # (1) First StorageClass — ports 1-4
provisioner: hspc.csi.hitachi.com
reclaimPolicy: Delete
volumeBindingMode: Immediate
allowVolumeExpansion: true
parameters:
  serialNumber: "112"               # (2) Storage serial number
  poolID: "1"                       # (3) HDP pool ID
  portID: CL1-A,CL2-A,CL3-A,CL4-A  # (4) 4 iSCSI ports only
  connectionType: iscsi
  csi.storage.k8s.io/fstype: ext4
  csi.storage.k8s.io/provisioner-secret-name: "secret-sample"
  csi.storage.k8s.io/provisioner-secret-namespace: "default"
  csi.storage.k8s.io/controller-publish-secret-name: "secret-sample"
  csi.storage.k8s.io/controller-publish-secret-namespace: "default"
  csi.storage.k8s.io/node-stage-secret-name: "secret-sample"
  csi.storage.k8s.io/node-stage-secret-namespace: "default"
  csi.storage.k8s.io/node-publish-secret-name: "secret-sample"
  csi.storage.k8s.io/node-publish-secret-namespace: "default"
  csi.storage.k8s.io/controller-expand-secret-name: "secret-sample"
  csi.storage.k8s.io/controller-expand-secret-namespace: "default"
```
Repeat with `vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-2` (ports CL5-A,CL6-A,CL7-A,CL8-A) and `vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-3` (ports CL9-A,CL10-A,CL11-A,CL12-A).
```bash
kubectl apply -f vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-1.yaml
kubectl apply -f vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-2.yaml
kubectl apply -f vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-3.yaml
```
### Create a MutatingAdmissionPolicy
The policy intercepts PVC creation on PVCs that carry the `storage.mymap.io/spread=true` label and whose name ends in a numeric ordinal (the StatefulSet naming convention). It extracts the ordinal, computes `ordinal % 3`, and selects the corresponding StorageClass via a JSONPatch mutation:
```yaml title="hspc-pvc-storageclass-rr-4.yaml"
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: MutatingAdmissionPolicy
metadata:
  name: hspc-pvc-storageclass-rr-4
spec:
  # keep Ignore while testing; switch to Fail after validation
  #failurePolicy: Ignore
  failurePolicy: Fail
  reinvocationPolicy: IfNeeded

  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      apiVersions: ["v1"]
      operations: ["CREATE"]
      resources: ["persistentvolumeclaims"]

  matchConditions:
  - name: opted-in
    # PVC must carry the spread label to be mutated
    expression: "has(object.metadata.labels) && object.metadata.labels['storage.mymap.io/spread'] == 'true'"
  - name: ordinal-suffix
    # PVC name must end in a numeric ordinal (e.g. data-mysts-0, data-mysts-1)
    expression: "object.metadata.name.matches('.*-[0-9]+$')"

  variables:
  - name: parts
    # Split the PVC name on '-' to isolate the ordinal at the end
    expression: "object.metadata.name.split('-')"
  - name: ordinal
    # Parse the last segment as an integer (the StatefulSet pod ordinal)
    expression: "int(variables.parts[variables.parts.size()-1])"
  - name: idx
    # Distribute evenly across 3 StorageClasses using modulo
    expression: "variables.ordinal % 3"
  - name: chosenSC
    expression: >
      variables.idx == 0 ? 'vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-1' :
      variables.idx == 1 ? 'vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-2' :
      variables.idx == 2 ? 'vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-3' :
                           'vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-4' :

  mutations:
  - patchType: "JSONPatch"
    jsonPatch:
      expression: >
        has(object.spec.storageClassName) ?
        [ JSONPatch{ op: "replace", path: "/spec/storageClassName", value: variables.chosenSC } ] :
        [ JSONPatch{ op: "add",     path: "/spec/storageClassName", value: variables.chosenSC } ]
```
```bash
kubectl apply -f hspc-pvc-storageclass-rr-4.yaml
```
### Create a MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding
The binding activates the policy using a two-layer opt-in: the **namespace** must carry `storage.mymap.io/spread-enabled=true` AND the individual **PVC** must carry `storage.mymap.io/spread=true`. Both selectors must match for the policy to fire:
```yaml title="hspc-pvc-storageclass-rr-4-binding.yaml"
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding
metadata:
  name: hspc-pvc-storageclass-rr-4-binding
spec:
  policyName: hspc-pvc-storageclass-rr-4   # Must match the MutatingAdmissionPolicy name
  matchResources:
    namespaceSelector:
      matchLabels:
        storage.mymap.io/spread-enabled: "true"   # (1) Namespace must opt in
    objectSelector:
      matchLabels:
        storage.mymap.io/spread: "true"           # (2) PVC must also carry the spread label
```
```bash
kubectl apply -f hspc-pvc-storageclass-rr-4-binding.yaml
```
### Label the namespace and verify StorageClasses
Label the target namespace to activate the MutatingAdmissionPolicy:
```bash
kubectl label namespace demo-test mutation-enabled=true
```
:::note
The binding manifest uses `storage.mymap.io/spread-enabled: "true"` as the `namespaceSelector` label. Ensure the label you apply here matches the `namespaceSelector.matchLabels` in your `MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding`.
:::
Verify the StorageClasses before deploying workloads:
```bash
root@rke2-cl2-ub-master1:~/jp-files# kubectl get sc
```
```
NAME                             PROVISIONER             RECLAIMPOLICY  VOLUMEBINDINGMODE  ALLOWVOLUMEEXPANSION  AGE
vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-12ports    hspc.csi.hitachi.com    Delete         Immediate          true                  18h
vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-1       hspc.csi.hitachi.com    Delete         Immediate          true                  27h
vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-2       hspc.csi.hitachi.com    Delete         Immediate          true                  27h
vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-3       hspc.csi.hitachi.com    Delete         Immediate          true                  18h
```
The original 12-port StorageClass remains present. The three 4-port StorageClasses (`4p-1`, `4p-2`, `4p-3`) will be used by the MAP for new PVC provisioning.
:::note
PVCs must also carry `storage.mymap.io/spread: "true"` on the object itself — add this label in your StatefulSet's `volumeClaimTemplates.metadata.labels` in the next step.
:::
Expected output:
```
vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-1   hspc.csi.hitachi.com   Delete   Immediate   true   ...
vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-2   hspc.csi.hitachi.com   Delete   Immediate   true   ...
vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-3   hspc.csi.hitachi.com   Delete   Immediate   true   ...
```
### Deploy a sample StatefulSet and verify PVC distribution
Two changes are required compared to a standard StatefulSet manifest:
1. **Add** `storage.mymap.io/spread: "true"` to both `spec.selector.matchLabels` and `spec.template.metadata.labels` — this satisfies the MAP's `objectSelector` in the binding
2. **Remove** the explicit `storageClassName` from `volumeClaimTemplates` — the MAP assigns it automatically based on the pod ordinal
```yaml title="demo-StatefulSet-app1-parallel-map.yaml"
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  namespace: demo-test
  name: hspc-pod-test-map
spec:
  podManagementPolicy: "Parallel"    # Provisions all PVCs concurrently — important at scale
  replicas: 6
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      name: hspc-pod-test-map
      storage.mymap.io/spread: "true"   # ADD: required for MAP objectSelector
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        name: hspc-pod-test-map
        storage.mymap.io/spread: "true"   # ADD: propagated to PVCs via volumeClaimTemplates
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: my-busybox
        image: registry.k8s.io/busybox
        volumeMounts:
        - mountPath: "/data"
          name: sample-volume
        command: ["sleep", "1000000"]
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: sample-volume
    spec:
      # storageClassName: vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-12ports  ← REMOVE this line; MAP assigns SC
      accessModes:
      - ReadWriteOnce
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 2Gi
```
```bash
kubectl apply -f demo-StatefulSet-app1-parallel-map.yaml
```
Scale the StatefulSet to 9 replicas to clearly observe the round-robin pattern across all three StorageClasses:
```bash
kubectl scale sts hspc-pod-test-map -n demo-test --replicas=9
```
Review the PVCs and confirm distribution:
```bash
kubectl get pvc -n demo-test
```
Expected output — 9 PVCs cycling across the three StorageClasses (ordinal % 3):
```
NAME                                STATUS   VOLUME                                     CAPACITY   ACCESS MODES   STORAGECLASS                   VOLUMEATTRIBUTESCLASS   AGE
sample-volume-hspc-pod-test-map-0   Bound    pvc-ac973e5-1123-4a30-9ed3-6b8cee7cb87a    2Gi        RWO            vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-1     <unset>                 3m26s
sample-volume-hspc-pod-test-map-1   Bound    pvc-fe2e31e2-b93e-46a2-b544-1932ca1d8080   2Gi        RWO            vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-2     <unset>                 3m26s
sample-volume-hspc-pod-test-map-2   Bound    pvc-2e2b5e3-0202-4587-b13d-4b4a0642d728    2Gi        RWO            vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-3     <unset>                 3m26s
sample-volume-hspc-pod-test-map-3   Bound    pvc-932f7957-1c0e-4096-91f5-5621bc1bc16a   2Gi        RWO            vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-1     <unset>                 3m26s
sample-volume-hspc-pod-test-map-4   Bound    pvc-4fcf9bc1-1a4d-4864-a605-c2e32664a8d5   2Gi        RWO            vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-2     <unset>                 3m26s
sample-volume-hspc-pod-test-map-5   Bound    pvc-d8b2f341-2b4a-4f9c-b318-7e5a9c2d1f84   2Gi        RWO            vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-3     <unset>                 3m26s
sample-volume-hspc-pod-test-map-6   Bound    pvc-9318319b-b9c2-4669-952a-aad43a2c06df   2Gi        RWO            vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-1     <unset>                 3m26s
sample-volume-hspc-pod-test-map-7   Bound    pvc-53a2f6a7-5701-4435-b0aa-a45dbe40561    2Gi        RWO            vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-2     <unset>                 3m26s
sample-volume-hspc-pod-test-map-8   Bound    pvc-a574e5a4-5bd2-406d-b537-4df2150aa30b   2Gi        RWO            vsp-5500-112-sc-iscsi-4p-3     <unset>                 3m26s
```

***

## Supporting More Than 256 Volumes per Host \{#more-than-256-volumes}

By default, HSPC supports up to **256 volumes per host**. This limitation exists because most Fibre Channel vendors maintain backward compatibility with the SCSI-2 protocol. Although SCSI-3 supports more volumes, many FC implementations still limit volumes per target to 256.

If your environment requires more than 256 volumes per target, configure the `SPC_LUN_MAX` environment variable. The maximum supported value is **2048**.

:::tip
For the HSPC configuration step to set `SPC_LUN_MAX`, see [Configuring the Maximum LUN Count](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/appendix.md#configuring-the-maximum-lun-count-spc_lun_max) in the HSPC Appendix.
:::

:::warning
Warning: These configuration changes require a host reboot to take effect. Applying this configuration will trigger or
require a reboot of the affected nodes.
:::

### OpenShift — MachineConfig (Recommended by Red Hat) \{#lun-boost-openshift}

Create one MachineConfig for each node role (worker and master):

```yaml title="99-worker-lun-boost.yaml"
apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
kind: MachineConfig
metadata:
  name: 99-worker-lun-boost
  labels:
    machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role: worker
spec:
  kernelArguments:
    - scsi_mod.max_luns=2048
    - lpfc.lpfc_max_luns=2048
  config:
    ignition:
      version: 3.2.0
    storage:
      files:
        - path: /etc/modprobe.d/lpfc.conf
          mode: 0644
          overwrite: true
          contents:
            source: data:text/plain;charset=utf-8;base64,b3B0aW9ucyBscGZjIGxwZmNfbWF4X2x1bnM9MjA0OA==
        - path: /etc/modprobe.d/scsi_mod.conf
          mode: 0644
          overwrite: true
          contents:
            source: data:text/plain;charset=utf-8;base64,b3B0aW9ucyBzY3NpX21vZCBtYXhfbHVucz0yMDQ4
```

```bash
oc apply -f 99-worker-lun-boost.yaml
# Verify rollout status
oc get mcp
oc describe mcp worker
```

### RKE2 — DaemonSet (No MachineConfig Available) \{#lun-boost-rke2}

RKE2 does not support MachineConfig. Apply the configuration using a DaemonSet:

```yaml title="rke2-lun-boost.yaml"
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: rke2-lun-boost
  namespace: kube-system
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: rke2-lun-boost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: rke2-lun-boost
    spec:
      hostPID: true
      hostNetwork: true
      containers:
        - name: modify-host
          image: registry.access.redhat.com/ubi9/ubi:latest
          securityContext:
            privileged: true
          command: ["/bin/bash", "-c"]
          args:
            - |
              echo "options scsi_mod max_luns=2048" > /host/etc/modprobe.d/scsi_mod.conf
              echo "options lpfc lpfc_max_luns=2048" > /host/etc/modprobe.d/lpfc.conf
              nsenter --mount=/host/proc/1/ns/mnt grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg || true
              sleep infinity
          volumeMounts:
            - name: host
              mountPath: /host
      volumes:
        - name: host
          hostPath:
            path: /
```

```bash
kubectl apply -f rke2-lun-boost.yaml
```

### RHEL Kubernetes — DaemonSet (Vanilla, Non-OpenShift, Non-RKE2) \{#lun-boost-rhel}

For vanilla Kubernetes deployments on RHEL:

```yaml title="lun-boost-ds.yaml"
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: lun-boost
  namespace: kube-system
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: lun-boost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: lun-boost
    spec:
      hostPID: true
      hostNetwork: true
      containers:
        - name: lun-boost
          image: registry.access.redhat.com/ubi9/ubi
          securityContext:
            privileged: true
          command: ["/bin/bash", "-c"]
          args:
            - |
              echo "options scsi_mod max_luns=2048" > /host/etc/modprobe.d/scsi_mod.conf
              echo "options lpfc lpfc_max_luns=2048" > /host/etc/modprobe.d/lpfc.conf
              nsenter --mount=/host/proc/1/ns/mnt grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg || true
              sleep infinity
          volumeMounts:
            - name: host
              mountPath: /host
      volumes:
        - name: host
          hostPath:
            path: /
```

```bash
kubectl apply -f lun-boost-ds.yaml
```



---
url: /container-plugins/csi-best-practices/disaster-recovery.md
---

# Disaster Recovery Best Practices

This section describes best practices for disaster recovery (DR) using Hitachi Replication Plug-in for Containers (HRPC) and the DR Operator, as well as high-availability architectures using multisite OpenShift Stretch Clusters with Stretch PVC.

***

## Hitachi Replication Plug-in for Containers (HRPC)

[HRPC](/container-plugins/hrpc/index.md) is the Hitachi CSI component for automating asynchronous and synchronous replication of volumes between clusters, supporting migration and disaster recovery use cases.

HRPC version 3.17 introduced the **DR Operator**, a Kubernetes-native disaster recovery orchestrator. The DR Operator automates and simplifies DR operations between primary and secondary storage systems, managing replication workflows such as:

- Split
- Failover
- Failback
- Resynchronization

These operations support TrueCopy (TC) and Universal Replicator (UR) pairs, ensuring minimal downtime and consistent data protection across sites.

:::note
HSPC is a required dependency for HRPC. Ensure that HSPC is configured before installing HRPC. See the [HSPC Installation Guide](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/installation.md).
:::

**Installation prerequisites:** Internet connectivity is required to the following registries:

- `registry.redhat.io`
- `registry.hitachivantara.com`
- `registry.k8s.io`

### Related Documentation

| Document                                                                                                                          | Description                                                                           |
| --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| [HRPC Documentation](/container-plugins/hrpc/index.md)                                                                            | Version index for HRPC — installation, replication operations, failover, and appendix |
| [HRPC DR Operator Guide](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/dr-operator.md)                                                          | Install and use the DR Operator for policy-based DR                                   |
| [HRPC Failover and Failback](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/failover-failback.md)                                                | Failover and failback procedures                                                      |
| [Product Compatibility Guide](https://compatibility.hitachivantara.com/products/hspc)                                             | HRPC compatibility matrix                                                             |
| [DR and Data Mobility in Hybrid Cloud Using HRPC](https://docs.hitachivantara.com/v/u/en-us/hitachi-integrated-systems/mk-sl-374) | Reference architecture for container and virtualization DR                            |

***

## Advantages of the DR Operator

The DR Operator provides key advantages over manual, per-PVC disaster recovery operations:

- **Eliminates manual, per-PVC operations** — Automates replication workflows at the application level.
- **Scalable DR** — Handles large Kubernetes environments with many PVCs.
- **User-defined policies** — Supports application-level DR policies.
- **Application-aware DR** — Performs failover and failback based on defined policies, preserving application consistency.

The DR Operator is analogous to VMware Site Recovery Manager (SRM), providing similar automated DR orchestration for container and virtualization workloads. For a reference architecture demonstrating DR for OpenShift Virtualization workloads, see:

- [Hitachi Integrated Solutions with Red Hat OpenShift Virtualization and VSP One Block](https://docs.hitachivantara.com/v/u/en-us/hitachi-integrated-systems/mk-sl-374)

***

## Disaster Recovery with the DR Operator

The DR Operator manages a group of PVCs defined as a DR policy. When a failover is triggered, the DR Operator:

1. Splits the replication pairs for the defined PVC group.
2. Promotes the secondary volumes to primary.
3. Reattaches the volumes in the secondary cluster.
4. Restores application availability at the DR site.

For failback, the DR Operator reverses the process and resynchronizes data to the original primary site.

:::tip
For detailed DR Operator configuration and workflow instructions, see the HRPC documentation and the [DR and Data Mobility reference architecture guide](https://docs.hitachivantara.com/v/u/en-us/hitachi-integrated-systems/mk-sl-374).
:::

***

## Multisite OpenShift Stretch Cluster and Stretch PVC \{#stretch-cluster}

Hitachi Vantara, in collaboration with Red Hat, has designed, validated, and implemented a modern high-availability (HA) architecture using a **stretched OpenShift cluster with VSP One Block**.

This solution combines:

- **Stretched storage** using Global-active device (GAD) technology
- **Hitachi Storage Plug-in for Containers (HSPC)** for automated provisioning and management of standard and stretched persistent volumes

### Architecture Overview

A stretched OpenShift cluster spans two data centers connected over a metro network. GAD maintains synchronous mirror copies of all data on both sides. HSPC automates the creation and lifecycle management of Stretch PVCs — PVCs that are transparently accessible from either site.

### Benefits

**High resiliency and business continuity for mission-critical applications:**

- **Zero downtime** in the event of a storage system failure or partial network failure
- **Minimal downtime** (a few minutes) in the event of host failure, total network failure, or site failure

**Application and data mobility and load balancing:**

- Live virtual machine migration between two data centers
- Seamless migration of stateful applications between two data centers

### Configuration Notes

- Stretch PVC pools can be created only on a **virtual storage machine (VSM)**.
- Mounting a PVC created **from** a Stretch PVC is not supported.
- Multi-AZ configuration is not supported by the cloud model of VSP One SDS Block.
- For GAD pair split and resync operations, use the `GadPairAction` custom resource introduced in HSPC v3.18.0. See [Stretched PVC](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/provisioning.md#stretched-pvc) in the HSPC Quick Reference.

### Related Resources

| Resource                                           | Link                                                                                                                               |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Community blog: OpenShift Stretch Cluster with VSP | [community.hitachivantara.com](https://community.hitachivantara.com/blogs/jeff-chen/2025/07/15/openshift-stretch-cluster-with-vsp) |
| Reference architecture guide                       | [Application-Optimized Solutions: MK-SL-383](https://docs.hitachivantara.com/v/u/en-us/application-optimized-solutions/mk-sl-383)  |
| HSPC Stretched PVC documentation                   | [Stretched PVC — HSPC Quick Reference](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.18.0/quick-reference/provisioning.md#stretched-pvc)              |



---
url: /container-plugins/csi-log-bundle.md
---

# Hitachi CSI Log Bundle

The [Hitachi CSI Log Bundle](https://github.com/cmccuistion-hv/Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle) is a **community-maintained** project (GPL-3.0) that helps you gather logs and cluster context for Hitachi CSI drivers in Kubernetes or OpenShift. It is **not** a Hitachi Vantara product. Use it alongside the official plug-in documentation when you need a single bundle for analysis or when opening a support case.

**Resources:**

- **Source and scripts:** [github.com/cmccuistion-hv/Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle](https://github.com/cmccuistion-hv/Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle)
- **Log viewer (hosted):** [cmccuistion-hv.github.io/Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle](https://cmccuistion-hv.github.io/Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle/) — open in a browser; no server install required
- **Authoritative CLI and release notes:** the repository [README](https://github.com/cmccuistion-hv/Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle/blob/main/README.md)

## What it does

The collector discovers the HSPC namespace and pods that use the `hspc-csi-sa` service account, then gathers:

- Container logs from those pods (per-container files, with a size limit per container)
- Cluster and namespace context (versions, nodes, manifests, events, pod descriptions)
- Optional **multipath** data per node
- Optional **DR-Operator** custom resources and related logs when present
- Optional **multi-cluster** collection (separate kubeconfigs for primary and secondary)
- Optional OpenShift **must-gather** output for Migration Toolkit for Containers (MTC) and/or Migration Toolkit for Virtualization (MTV)

For exact behavior and options, see the upstream README.

## Requirements and installation

**Bash (`get_hitachicsilogs.sh`):** Linux or macOS, Bash 4.0+, `kubectl` or `oc` in PATH (or next to the script), valid kubeconfig. Optional: `zip` or `python3` for compression.

**PowerShell (`get_hitachicsilogs.ps1`):** Windows, Linux, or macOS, PowerShell 5.1+ or PowerShell Core 7+, `kubectl`/`oc`, valid kubeconfig.

Clone the repository and make the shell script executable:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/cmccuistion-hv/Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle.git
cd Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle
chmod +x get_hitachicsilogs.sh
```

## Running the collector

Examples below are illustrative. For the full option list and defaults, use `-h` / `--help` on the script or read the [README tables](https://github.com/cmccuistion-hv/Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle/blob/main/README.md#command-line-options).

**Bash — basic (default kubeconfig):**

```bash
./get_hitachicsilogs.sh
```

**Bash — specific kubeconfig, OpenShift `oc`, and namespace:**

```bash
./get_hitachicsilogs.sh --kubeconfig /path/to/kubeconfig --oc -n hspc-system
```

**Bash — multi-cluster (DR-style environments):**

```bash
./get_hitachicsilogs.sh \
  --kubeconfig-primary /path/to/primary-kubeconfig \
  --kubeconfig-secondary /path/to/secondary-kubeconfig
```

**Bash — skip zip or skip multipath collection:**

```bash
./get_hitachicsilogs.sh --no-compress
./get_hitachicsilogs.sh --no-multipath
```

**Bash — optional must-gather (OpenShift + `oc` only; can take a long time):**

```bash
./get_hitachicsilogs.sh --mtc
./get_hitachicsilogs.sh --mtv
```

**PowerShell — equivalent patterns:**

```powershell
.\get_hitachicsilogs.ps1
.\get_hitachicsilogs.ps1 -Kubeconfig C:\path\to\kubeconfig -Oc -Namespace hspc-system
.\get_hitachicsilogs.ps1 -KubeconfigPrimary C:\primary -KubeconfigSecondary C:\secondary
.\get_hitachicsilogs.ps1 -NoCompress
.\get_hitachicsilogs.ps1 -NoCollectMultipath
.\get_hitachicsilogs.ps1 -Mtc -Mtv
```

**Line endings on Linux:** If you see `bash\r: No such file or directory`, convert CRLF to LF, for example: `sed -i 's/\r$//' get_hitachicsilogs.sh`.

## Bundle contents

- **Per-container logs:** files named like `<pod-name>_<container-name>.log` (collection is capped per container, typically up to hundreds of MB — see README).
- **`cluster-context.txt`:** cluster version, platform detection, node details, HSPC custom resource YAML, workload manifests in the namespace, pod ownership, `kubectl describe` style output, recent events, and DR-Operator CRs when detected.
- **`errors.log` / `failed-pods.txt`:** collection-time errors and any pods or containers that could not be collected.
- **Multipath (unless skipped):** under `multipath/`, per-node capture of `/etc/multipath.conf` and `multipath -ll`. On **OpenShift**, collection uses `oc debug node/<node>`. On **Kubernetes**, the script uses short-lived privileged Jobs with host path mounts; you need permission to create/delete Jobs in the HSPC namespace. Optional environment variables: `MULTIPATH_JOB_IMAGE` (default `ubuntu:22.04`), `MULTIPATH_JOB_TIMEOUT` (default `120` seconds).
- **Must-gather (if requested):** `must-gather-mtc/` and/or `must-gather-mtv/` when supported; skipped with a warning if not on OpenShift or without `oc`.

The script verifies the `hspcs.csi.hitachi.com` CRD exists before collecting.

## Using the log viewer

You can use the **[integrated Log Bundle Viewer](/container-plugins/csi-log-bundle-viewer.md)** built into this site — no download required. Load a **ZIP** archive or a **folder** (drag-and-drop or file picker) directly in your browser. Alternatively, download **`Hitachi-CSI-log-Bundle-Viewer.html`** from the [Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle](https://github.com/cmccuistion-hv/Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle) repository and open it locally.

Screenshots below are from **Hitachi CSI Log Bundle Viewer** (example: v1.7.0). Your build may differ slightly.

### Interface overview

The viewer is organised into a **header**, a **left sidebar**, and a **main** pane.

- **Header:** Title and version, how many containers are loaded, and a link to **GitHub Releases**.
- **Cluster View:** When the bundle includes more than one cluster, **Primary** and **Secondary** (or equivalent) toggles switch which cluster’s data drives the summary, container list, and tabs.
- **Sidebar — Summary:** Bundle file name, capture time, collection script version, **total errors / warnings / log lines**, **platform** (for example OpenShift or Kubernetes) and version, HSPC/HRPC/DR-Operator-related versions when present, git or build metadata if captured, and **node** count.
- **Sidebar — Containers:** One row per container in the bundle with counts for warnings, errors, and log lines. Selecting a row focuses log and context views on that container (where applicable).
- **Main tabs:** **Logs**, **Health & Diagnostics**, **Configuration**, **Cluster Info**, **DR Policies** (when DR CRDs exist in the bundle), and **AI Analysis** (optional feature).

### Logs tab

The **Logs** tab filters the selected container’s log stream. Use **level** toggles (for example ERROR, WARNING, INFO, ALL), a **search** box, and optional **time range** controls. Actions such as line wrap, copy, and export depend on the viewer build. Log lines are syntax-highlighted (timestamps, severities, and message text).

![Hitachi CSI Log Bundle Viewer: Logs tab with sidebar summary, container list, level filters, search, time range, and colorized log output for a selected CSI container.](/images/csi-log-bundle/log-viewer-logs-tab.png)
### Health & Diagnostics

**Health & Diagnostics** summarises the bundle with cards (for example total pods, nodes running HSPC pods, aggregate errors and warnings) and an **error categories** table. Categories group issues such as configuration, connectivity, device detection, and DR policy reconciliation. Selecting a row can open related log context (behaviour may vary by release).

![Hitachi CSI Log Bundle Viewer: Health & Diagnostics tab with summary cards for pods, nodes, errors, and warnings, plus an error categories table with counts and affected containers.](/images/csi-log-bundle/log-viewer-health-diagnostics.png)
### Configuration

The **Configuration** tab combines high-level counts with drill-down tables. Typical elements include:

- **Summary cards** — for example VSP storage arrays, storage classes, nodes with WWN data, total volumes, storage ports in use, and multipath configs (selecting a card may focus detail below).
- **Storage array** table — serial numbers, related storage classes, ports, and status.
- **Hosts, WWNs, multipath** table — Kubernetes node names, WWPN/WWNN, related pods, and whether multipath data was found; expanding or following **View** shows captured **`/etc/multipath.conf`** and **`multipath -ll`** text when the collector included multipath.

For **multi-cluster** bundles, the viewer can highlight differences between clusters (diff-style presentation depends on release).

![Hitachi CSI Log Bundle Viewer: Configuration tab with summary metric cards for arrays, storage classes, nodes, volumes, ports, and multipath; tables for VSP arrays and hosts with multipath expanders.](/images/csi-log-bundle/log-viewer-configuration-summary.png)
![Hitachi CSI Log Bundle Viewer: Configuration tab showing Hosts WWNs Multipath table and expanded multipath.conf and multipath -ll output for a node.](/images/csi-log-bundle/log-viewer-configuration-multipath.png)
### Cluster Info

The **Cluster Info** tab (not shown in the figures below) presents additional cluster and bundle metadata from `cluster-context.txt` and related files. Use it when you need raw context alongside the structured tabs above.

### DR Policies and replication (when data is present)

When the bundle contains DR-Operator custom resources, the **DR Policies** tab lists policies and supports **side-by-side** comparison per cluster (namespace, spec fields such as desired state, replication attribute, mode, journal IDs, PVC selectors, and **status**). Per-policy actions may include **Logs**, **Replications** (with counts), **View YAML**, and cluster-scoped shortcuts. This aligns with inspecting `replicationType`, `desiredPairState`, `storageClassName`, and status fields such as `volumeStatus`, `deviceGroupName`, `copyGroupName`, and `copyPairName` in the underlying CRs.

![Hitachi CSI Log Bundle Viewer: DR Policies tab with a policy card comparing primary and secondary cluster spec and status, and buttons for Logs, Replications, and YAML.](/images/csi-log-bundle/log-viewer-dr-policies.png)
### AI-assisted analysis (optional)

Under **AI Analysis**, **Setup** is where you configure an OpenAI-compatible **API key**, **base URL**, and **model**. **Analysis** runs a bundle-wide prompt: the viewer sends a structured payload (environment summary, error categories, identifiers, sampled lines, config excerpts, pod/node health, and similar) and renders **priority-ranked** finding cards with category, evidence, suggested actions, and root-cause hints. The UI may show how many tokens were sent for transparency.

**Chat** keeps a thread: you ask follow-up questions and the viewer attaches relevant log excerpts. Typical actions include **Send**, **Copy**, **Save**, and **Clear chat**.

![Hitachi CSI Log Bundle Viewer: AI Analysis tab on Analysis sub-tab showing Analyze with AI and ranked finding cards with suggested actions and evidence.](/images/csi-log-bundle/log-viewer-ai-analysis.png)
![Hitachi CSI Log Bundle Viewer: AI Analysis tab on Chat sub-tab with conversation history, token notice, and Ask a question input with Send Copy Save Clear chat.](/images/csi-log-bundle/log-viewer-ai-chat.png)
**Privacy:** Your API key and any data sent for analysis go **only to the endpoint you configure**, not to Hitachi Vantara. Do not enable this feature if your bundle contains secrets or if your organisation forbids sending diagnostic text to external APIs.

## Troubleshooting the collector

Common issues are documented in the repository README, including:

- `kubectl` / `oc` not found
- CRD `hspcs.csi.hitachi.com` not found (wrong cluster or HSPC not installed)
- No HSPC pods found (check namespace and service account `hspc-csi-sa`)
- Slow collection or large logs
- Must-gather skipped (OpenShift / `oc` required)
- Permission errors (RBAC; cluster-admin may be needed for full collection)

Report tool bugs or request features via [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/cmccuistion-hv/Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle/issues). For Hitachi plug-in product support, use your normal [Hitachi Vantara support](https://support.hitachivantara.com/en_us/contact-us.html) channel and the official [HSPC](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.17.4/quick-reference/troubleshooting.md) / [HRPC](/container-plugins/hrpc/v3.17.4/troubleshooting.md) troubleshooting guides.

## License

The Hitachi CSI Log Bundle project is licensed under **GNU General Public License v3.0**. See the [LICENSE](https://github.com/cmccuistion-hv/Hitachi-CSI-Log-Bundle/blob/main/LICENSE) file in the repository.



---
url: /container-plugins/csi-log-bundle-viewer.md
---

---

# HSPC v3.16.1 Overview

Version: 3.16.1
Doc: MK-92ADPTR142-22
Applies to: HSPC 3.16.0 / 3.16.1

Hitachi Storage Plug-in for Containers (HSPC) is a Kubernetes **Container Storage Interface (CSI) driver** for Hitachi VSP storage systems. It enables dynamic provisioning of Persistent Volumes, volume snapshots, clones, volume expansion, Stretched PVC (GAD), and resource partitioning directly from Kubernetes or OpenShift.

HSPC is also the **required foundation** for HRPC (replication) and HSPP (monitoring).

## What's New in 3.16.x

Version 3.16.x introduced and refined:

- **VSP One SDS Block** support — software-defined block storage integration
- **Stretched PVC (Global Active Device)** — high-availability active-active replication across two sites
- **Resource partitioning** — isolate storage resources per Kubernetes cluster
- **Static provisioning** — use existing storage volumes as PVCs
- **ReadWriteMany** support for block volumes (OpenShift Virtualization)
- **NVMe over FC** and **NVMe/TCP** connectivity (bare metal)

## Supported Platforms

| Component                          | Supported Versions     |
| ---------------------------------- | ---------------------- |
| Red Hat OpenShift                  | 4.17, 4.18, 4.19       |
| Kubernetes                         | 1.30, 1.31, 1.32, 1.33 |
| Rancher Kubernetes Engine 2 (RKE2) | 1.30, 1.31, 1.32, 1.33 |
| Amazon EKS                         | 1.30, 1.31, 1.32, 1.33 |
| CPU architecture                   | x86\_64                |

## Storage Systems

| Storage Family       | Notes                                                       |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| VSP 5000 series      | Full support                                                |
| VSP E series         | Full support                                                |
| VSP F350/370/700/900 | Full support                                                |
| VSP G350/370/700/900 | Full support                                                |
| VSP One Block        | Full support                                                |
| VSP One SDS Block    | Supported; some features restricted (see notes per section) |
| VSP One B20          | Supported; resource partitioning and cloning not available  |

## Documentation

Use the sidebar to navigate the Quick Reference Guide sections, or jump directly to a topic:

- [Introduction](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/index.md) — Scope and conventions
- [Overview](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/overview.md) — Architecture, requirements, and pre-installation
- [Installation](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/installation.md) — Deploy via OperatorHub (OpenShift) or Kubernetes operator
- [Configuration](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/configuration.md) — HSPC instance configuration and environment variables
- [Storage Classes](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/storage-classes.md) — StorageClass YAML reference, resource partitioning, Stretched PVC
- [Provisioning Volumes](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/provisioning.md) — PVC, Pod settings, command examples, raw block, RWX
- [Volume Snapshots](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/snapshots.md) — Snapshot and clone procedures
- [Expanding Volumes](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/expanding-volumes.md) — Online volume expansion
- [Upgrade & Uninstall](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/uninstalling.md) — Upgrade, re-creation, and uninstallation procedures
- [Troubleshooting](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/troubleshooting.md) — Diagnostics and known issues
- [Appendix](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/appendix.md) — Environment variables, static provisioning reference

:::tip
For the latest version of HSPC, see [HSPC v3.17.4](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.17.4/index.md).
:::



---
url: /container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/appendix.md
---

# Appendix

Version: 3.16.1
Chapter: 3 (Usage — Static Provisioning & Stretched PVC operations)

## Static Provisioning

Static provisioning allows existing storage system volumes to be used as PVCs without HSPC creating new storage. This is useful for migrating existing workloads to Kubernetes.

:::note
Static provisioning is **not supported** for Stretched PVCs.
:::

:::note
You can create only **one PV for one volume**. If you create more than one PV for the same volume, the functions supported by HSPC might not work properly.
:::

### Requirements

**VSP Family and VSP One Block:**

- The LDEV has the **DP attribute** (Dynamic Provisioning)
  - VSP family: LDEV does **not** have the DRS attribute
  - VSP One Block: LDEV **does** have the DRS attribute
- LDEV nickname format: `spc-<10-digit-hex>` (must be unique per LDEV)
  ```bash
  echo spc-$(cat /dev/urandom | tr -dc a-f0-9 | head -c 10)
  ```
- The LDEV is **not mapped** to any port
- **No pairs** are formed on the LDEV

**VSP One SDS Block:**

- `naaId` is available as volume information (volumes created with v1.11 or earlier are not supported)
- `volumeType` is `Normal`
- `name` and `nickname` have the same value in format `spc-<10-digit-hex>`
- The volume is not connected to a computer node

### Procedure

1. Create a Secret and StorageClass (see [Storage Classes](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/storage-classes.md))
2. Create the PV (see below)
3. Create a PVC that references the PV

### Creating a PV — VSP Family and VSP One Block

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: static-pv
  annotations:
    pv.kubernetes.io/provisioned-by: hspc.csi.hitachi.com   # Required if reclaimPolicy: Delete
spec:
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Delete    # Delete or Retain
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce
  capacity:
    storage: 1Gi                           # Must match the actual LDEV size
  volumeMode: Filesystem                   # Filesystem or Block
  csi:
    fsType: ext4
    volumeAttributes:
      connectionType: fc                   # fc, iscsi, or nvme-fc
      ports: CL1-A, CL2-A                  # Required for fc or iscsi; not needed for nvme-fc
      # portIPs: "192.168.10.10, 192.168.10.11"  # Required for iSCSI when the LDEV is assigned to a specific resource group
    volumeHandle: 01--scsi--900000070010--50000--spc-c3d46c5a71
    #  Format: 01--<io-protocol>--<storage-device-id>--<ldev-id-decimal>--<ldev-nickname>
    #  io-protocol: "scsi" for fc/iscsi, "nvme" for nvme-fc
    #  storage-device-id: 12-digit value (e.g., 6-digit-fixed-prefix + 6-digit-serial)
    driver: hspc.csi.hitachi.com
    controllerExpandSecretRef:
      name: secret-sample
      namespace: default
    controllerPublishSecretRef:
      name: secret-sample
      namespace: default
  storageClassName: sc-sample
  claimRef:
    name: static-pvc      # PVC name — must match the PVC you will create
    namespace: default
```

:::caution
`volumeMode` defaults to `Filesystem`. If the LDEV was used as a **raw block volume**, specify `volumeMode: Block`. Specifying `Filesystem` for an LDEV that was used as a raw block volume will format it as a file system, **deleting existing data**.
:::

### volumeHandle Format

```
01--<io-protocol>--<storage-device-id>--<ldev-id>--<ldev-nickname>
```

| Field               | Description                                                                                                                            |
| ------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `io-protocol`       | `scsi` for FC or iSCSI; `nvme` for NVMe over FC                                                                                        |
| `storage-device-id` | 12-digit value: 6-digit fixed prefix for the storage model + 6-digit serial number. Check via REST API Reference Guide for each model. |
| `ldev-id`           | Decimal LDEV ID — check via storage management software                                                                                |
| `ldev-nickname`     | LDEV nickname — check via storage management software                                                                                  |

:::note
The default `persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy` is **`Retain`**. When `Retain` is set, the PV and LDEV are **not** deleted when the PVC is deleted — the PV enters a Released state. To reuse the LDEV, you must re-create the PV. If you want the PV and LDEV deleted automatically when the PVC is deleted, specify `Delete` (and also set the `pv.kubernetes.io/provisioned-by` annotation).
:::

### Creating a PV — VSP One SDS Block

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: static-pv
  annotations:
    pv.kubernetes.io/provisioned-by: hspc.csi.hitachi.com
spec:
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Delete
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce
  capacity:
    storage: 1Gi
  volumeMode: Filesystem
  csi:
    fsType: ext4
    volumeAttributes:
      connectionType: fc
    volumeHandle: 60060e8116602000601660200000004f--spc-909b93359a--4026f840-e15e-4410-a37f-3862072c10ba
    # Format: <volume-naaId>--<volume-name>--<volume-id>
    driver: hspc.csi.hitachi.com
    controllerExpandSecretRef:
      name: secret-sample
      namespace: default
    controllerPublishSecretRef:
      name: secret-sample
      namespace: default
  storageClassName: sc-sample-vsp-one-sds-block
  claimRef:
    name: static-pvc
    namespace: default
```

### Creating a PVC for Static Provisioning

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: static-pvc
  namespace: default
spec:
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
  storageClassName: sc-sample
  volumeMode: Filesystem
  volumeName: static-pv    # Must match the PV name
```

```bash
kubectl apply -f <pv-yaml-file>
kubectl apply -f <pvc-yaml-file>

# Confirm the PVC is Bound
kubectl get pvc static-pvc
```

### Static Provisioning Troubleshooting

**VolumeAttachment remains after Pod deletion**

If Pod creation fails due to incorrect PV parameters, deleting the Pod may leave a VolumeAttachment object. Remove it:

```bash
kubectl patch volumeattachments <va-name> --type merge -p '{"metadata":{"finalizers":null}}'
```

Causes: incorrect `volumeHandle` format (`0x0000c002`), insufficient LDEV permissions (`0x00001007`), or missing `controllerPublishSecretRef` (`0x0000c00f`).

**PV remains after PVC deletion**

If PV parameters are incorrect and `reclaimPolicy: Delete` is set, the PV may remain after PVC deletion:

```bash
kubectl get pv <pv-name> -o yaml   # Get volumeHandle to identify the LDEV
kubectl delete pv <pv-name>
# Then delete the LDEV via storage management software if needed
```

**Volume remains after PVC deletion**

If LDEV parameters don't match the actual LDEV (incorrect `ldev-nickname` or insufficient user permissions), the LDEV is not deleted. Manually delete via storage management software.

***

## Stretched PVC — Additional Operations

### Checking Stretched PVC Volume Attributes

```bash
kubectl describe pv <pv-name>
# Look in spec.csi.volumeAttributes
```

| Attribute           | Description                        |
| ------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| `nickname`          | LDEV nickname (same at both sites) |
| `primarySerial`     | Primary storage serial number      |
| `primaryVolumeID`   | Primary site LDEV ID               |
| `secondarySerial`   | Secondary storage serial number    |
| `secondaryVolumeID` | Secondary site LDEV ID             |

### Deleting a Stretched PVC

Delete the PVC as normal:

```bash
kubectl delete pvc <stretched-pvc-name>
```

HSPC deletes both the primary and secondary LDEVs and removes the GAD pair. Use Storage Navigator to verify the pair is removed.

### Forcibly Deleting a Stretched PVC

If a site failure or GAD pair failure prevents normal PVC deletion, the delete operation will hang. To force-delete a Stretched PVC:

1. Check the information about the resources created at the primary and secondary sites using `kubectl describe pv <pv-name>` (see [Checking Stretched PVC Volume Attributes](#checking-stretched-pvc-volume-attributes)).
2. Delete the PVC and the PV:

```bash
kubectl patch pvc <pvc-name> --type merge -p '{"metadata":{"finalizers":null}}'
kubectl delete pvc <pvc-name>
kubectl delete pv <pv-name>
```

3. Based on the information checked in step 1, use Storage Navigator to delete the global-active device pair, volumes, and the associated LUNs. Then change the virtual LDEV IDs of the deleted volumes to **unassigned (65534)**.

### Failover

If the primary site fails:

1. Confirm the failure using Storage Navigator.
2. Perform a GAD failover to promote the secondary site. Follow the procedures in the **Global-Active Device User Guide**.
3. After failover, the Stretched PVC continues operating from the (now primary) secondary site.

HSPC does not manage or automate failover. The cluster administrator is responsible for monitoring the GAD pair status via Storage Navigator.



---
url: /container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/configuration.md
---

# Configuring HSPC

Version: 3.16.1
Chapter: 2 (continued)

HSPC is configured by editing the **CustomResource YAML file** (`hspc_v1_hspc.yaml`). This file controls the HSPC controller and node pod configurations.

## HSPC Instance Parameters

| Parameter                                    | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `spec.imagePullSecrets`                      | Specify if a Secret is required to pull images from a private registry.                                                                                                                                      |
| `spec.controller.containers.name`            | Name of the container to configure in `hspc-csi-controller` pods (e.g., `hspc-csi-driver`, `csi-provisioner`). Use `kubectl describe deployment hspc-csi-controller -n <namespace>` to list container names. |
| `spec.controller.containers.image`           | Image name for `hspc-csi-controller`.                                                                                                                                                                        |
| `spec.controller.containers.imagePullPolicy` | Image pull policy for `hspc-csi-controller`. Default: `IfNotPresent`.                                                                                                                                        |
| `spec.controller.containers.env`             | Environment variables for the `hspc-csi-controller` container. See [Environment Variables](#environment-variables).                                                                                          |
| `spec.controller.containers.args`            | Entry-point arguments for `hspc-csi-controller`. Replaces all parameters in `spec.template.spec.containers.args`.                                                                                            |
| `spec.controller.tolerations`                | Toleration settings for the `hspc-csi-controller` pod. Uses standard Kubernetes format.                                                                                                                      |
| `spec.controller.affinity.nodeAffinity`      | Node affinity for the `hspc-csi-controller` pod.                                                                                                                                                             |
| `spec.node.containers.name`                  | Name of the container to configure in `hspc-csi-node` pods (e.g., `hspc-csi-driver`, `liveness-probe`). Use `kubectl describe daemonset hspc-csi-node -n <namespace>` to list container names.               |
| `spec.node.containers.image`                 | Image name for `hspc-csi-node`.                                                                                                                                                                              |
| `spec.node.containers.imagePullPolicy`       | Image pull policy for `hspc-csi-node`. Default: `IfNotPresent`.                                                                                                                                              |
| `spec.node.containers.env`                   | Environment variables for the `hspc-csi-node` container.                                                                                                                                                     |
| `spec.node.containers.args`                  | Entry-point arguments for `hspc-csi-node`.                                                                                                                                                                   |
| `spec.node.tolerations`                      | Toleration settings for the `hspc-csi-node` pods.                                                                                                                                                            |
| `spec.node.affinity.nodeAffinity`            | Node affinity for `hspc-csi-node` pods.                                                                                                                                                                      |

## Environment Variables

The following environment variables apply to the `hspc-csi-driver` container in `hspc-csi-controller`:

| Variable                 | Description                                                                            | Default |
| ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------- |
| `SPC_VERIFY_CERTIFICATE` | When `true`, validates the TLS certificate of the storage system in HTTPS connections. | `false` |
| `TZ`                     | Timezone for log timestamps (e.g., `Asia/Tokyo`).                                      | `UTC`   |

### Example: Enabling TLS Certificate Verification

1. Check the current environment variable settings:

```bash
kubectl get deployment -n <hspc-namespace> hspc-csi-controller -o yaml
# Look for the hspc-csi-driver container's env section
```

2. Add `SPC_VERIFY_CERTIFICATE` to the HSPC CustomResource:

```yaml
apiVersion: csi.hitachi.com/v1
kind: HSPC
metadata:
  name: hspc
  namespace: <hspc-namespace>
spec:
  controller:
    containers:
      - name: hspc-csi-driver
        env:
        - name: SPC_VERIFY_CERTIFICATE
          value: "true"
```

3. Uninstall and reinstall HSPC to apply the change. See [Upgrade & Uninstall](/container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/uninstalling.md).

4. Verify the change was applied:

```bash
kubectl get deployment -n <hspc-namespace> hspc-csi-controller -o yaml
# Confirm SPC_VERIFY_CERTIFICATE: "true" appears in the env section
```

### Example: Limiting Concurrent Worker Threads

To reduce storage overload when many PVCs are created or deleted simultaneously, limit the `--worker-threads` argument on the `csi-provisioner` container. The default is 20.

```yaml
apiVersion: csi.hitachi.com/v1
kind: HSPC
metadata:
  name: hspc
  namespace: <hspc-namespace>
spec:
  controller:
    containers:
      - name: csi-provisioner
        args:
          - --worker-threads=10
```

Errors that may be reduced by lowering this value: `0x0000100b`, `0x0000100f`, `0x0000101a`, `0x0000f007`.



---
url: /container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/expanding-volumes.md
---

# Expanding Volumes

Version: 3.16.1
Chapter: 3 (Usage)

HSPC supports **online volume expansion** — you can expand a PVC without deleting and recreating the Pod.

**Prerequisites:** The StorageClass must have `allowVolumeExpansion: true`.

:::caution

- Confirm expansion is complete using `kubectl get pvc` (CAPACITY column) before shutting down the OS or draining the node.
- **NVMe over FC and NVMe/TCP:** Volume expansion while the PVC is attached to a host is **not supported**. Detach the PVC first (delete the Pod), expand, then re-attach.

:::

## Expansion Restrictions

| Restriction                     | Details                                                                                                                     |
| ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Minimum increment               | 1 GiB                                                                                                                       |
| Maximum increment per operation | 7 TiB, or the pool warning threshold — whichever is lower. If you need more than 7 TiB, run the command again.              |
| Capacity reduction              | Not supported                                                                                                               |
| StorageClass restriction        | If `allowVolumeExpansion: false`, the PVC cannot be expanded                                                                |
| PV size command                 | `kubectl get pv <name> -o yaml` does **not** update after expansion. Use `kubectl get pv <name>` (CAPACITY column) instead. |

## Expand a PVC

Use `kubectl patch` to request the new size:

```bash
# Expand pvc-sample to 5 GiB
kubectl patch pvc pvc-sample --patch \
  '{"spec":{"resources":{"requests":{"storage": "5Gi"}}}}'
```

Verify completion:

```bash
kubectl get pv <pv-name>
# NAME       CAPACITY   ACCESS MODES   RECLAIM POLICY   STATUS   CLAIM              STORAGECLASS
# <pv-name>  5Gi        RWO            Delete           Bound    default/pvc-sample sc-sample
```

:::caution
Always use `kubectl` to resize LDEVs created by HSPC. Do not resize them directly in the storage management software.

If the PVC is not attached to a Pod, the capacity increases but the PVC capacity display may not update until the volume is mounted and the filesystem is expanded from within the node.
:::

## Expanding a Stretched PVC

Volume expansion for Stretched PVCs follows the same procedure as regular PVC expansion. HSPC expands both the primary and secondary GAD volumes and updates the pair.

Before expanding a Stretched PVC, verify the following:

:::note

- Verify that `allowVolumeExpansion` is set to `true` in the StorageClass. If set to `false`, the volume cannot be expanded.
- Verify that the GAD pair is **healthy and in Pair state** before and during expansion.
- The requested storage size must be greater than the current PVC capacity for the expansion to succeed.
  :::



---
url: /container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/index.md
---

# Introduction

Document: MK-92ADPTR142-22
Applies to: HSPC 3.16.0 / 3.16.1

This Quick Reference Guide provides an implementation overview and describes the usage requirements, installation, and configuration of **Hitachi Storage Plug-in for Containers (HSPC)**.

Please read this document carefully to understand how to install and use the plug-in, and maintain a copy for your reference.

## About This Guide

Hitachi Storage Plug-in for Containers lets you create containers and run stateful applications inside those containers by using Hitachi storage volumes as dynamically provisioned Persistent Volumes.

This guide covers:

- Requirements for installing and configuring HSPC
- Installation procedures for OpenShift and Kubernetes
- StorageClass and Secret configuration
- Volume provisioning, snapshots, cloning, and expansion
- Stretched PVC (GAD) and resource partitioning
- Upgrade, re-creation, and uninstallation
- Troubleshooting guidance

## Conventions for Capacity Values

This document uses the following conventions for capacity values:

| Unit             | Value       |
| ---------------- | ----------- |
| 1 KB (kilobyte)  | 1,000 bytes |
| 1 MB (megabyte)  | 1,000 KB    |
| 1 GB (gigabyte)  | 1,000 MB    |
| 1 TB (terabyte)  | 1,000 GB    |
| 1 KiB (kibibyte) | 1,024 bytes |
| 1 MiB (mebibyte) | 1,024 KiB   |
| 1 GiB (gibibyte) | 1,024 MiB   |
| 1 TiB (tebibyte) | 1,024 GiB   |

## Storage Model Abbreviations

| Abbreviation      | Full Name                                                                                                |
| ----------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| VSP family        | Hitachi Virtual Storage Platform family (collective for VSP E series, VSP F/G/N series, VSP 5000 series) |
| VSP E series      | VSP E590, E790, E990, E1090 (and H variants)                                                             |
| VSP 5000 series   | VSP 5100, 5200, 5500, 5600, 5100H, 5500H, 5600H                                                          |
| VSP One Block     | VSP One Block 24, 26, 28                                                                                 |
| VSP One B20       | Collective name for VSP One Block 24, 26, 28                                                             |
| VSP One SDS Block | Hitachi Virtual Storage Platform One SDS Block                                                           |

## Getting Help

| Resource               | Details                                                                   |
| ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Support Portal         | [support.hitachivantara.com](https://support.hitachivantara.com)          |
| Community              | [community.hitachivantara.com](https://community.hitachivantara.com)      |
| Documentation Feedback | [doc.feedback@hitachivantara.com](mailto:doc.feedback@hitachivantara.com) |

:::tip
Read the release notes before installing. They may contain requirements or restrictions not fully described in this guide.
:::



---
url: /container-plugins/hspc/v3.16.1/quick-reference/installation.md
---

# Installing HSPC

Version: 3.16.1
Chapter: 2

The installation method depends on whether your environment is OpenShift or Kubernetes. Both methods use the HSPC **Operator**, which manages the lifecycle of HSPC components.

:::warning
If a previous version of HSPC is installed, **remove it before starting the installation procedure.**
:::

## Installation on OpenShift
